title: Accounting I: Introduction to Financial Accounting
description: Financial accounting deals with the preparation, presentation, and analysis of financial statements. The course involves the study of the terminology and conceptual framework underlying the measurement, processing, and communication of financial information to external and internal users. The course is required of all business and management majors, but is open to any student with an interest in learning this essential part of the language of business.

title: Advanced Accounting
description: This course studies the problems related to such specialized topics as partnerships, consignment and installment sales, agency and branch operations, consolidations and business combinations, foreign operations, fiduciaries, and non-profit organizations.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Auditing
description: An intensive examination of the methods and procedures used to obtain necessary evidence to form an independent professional opinion regarding the fair representation of clients' financial statements. Topics include the ethical and legal responsibilities of the auditor, evaluation of internal controls, the use of sampling techniques, and current AICPA auditing publications.

title: Accounting Information Systems and Internal Controls
description: This course will explore the business accounting processes and internal controls, with emphasis on ethics and corporate governance. The course will also cover the use of data flow diagrams and document flow charts to understand these business processes and evaluate internal controls. Lastly, students will learn how to use data analysis applications to aid in making real business decisions using this technology.

title: Cost Accounting II
description: This course is a continuation of ACC 301. Topics covered include absorption and variable costing, cost-volume-profit analysis, cost control, activity-based costing and other innovative techniques, transfer pricing decisions, performance evaluation, relevant costing, capital asset selection and capital budgeting, and quantitative planning tools, such as linear programming and regression analysis.

title: Income Tax Accounting I
description: This course is designed for all students who have completed the introductory accounting courses ( ACC-101 & ACC-102 ) and who have an interest in federal income tax matters concerning the individual taxpayer. After an introduction to the federal tax system in the United States, topics include: gross income, deductions allowable in determining adjusted gross income and taxable income, special tax computations and credits, recognition and non-recognition of gains and losses, basis rules, special treatment of capital assets, and administrative provisions of the tax law.

title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: This course is designed and required for the major in accounting. An in-depth study is made of the form, content, and objectives of corporate financial reporting. It includes controversial issues involved in revenue recognition, inventory evaluation, depreciation theory, and intangible assets, together with the impact these matters have on the determination of corporate earnings.

title: Income Tax Accounting II
description: This course is devoted to the taxation of corporations, partnerships, and fiduciaries. Topics include: transactions between partners and partnerships for retirement, death, transfer or partial liquidation of an interest; special deductions for corporations; Subchapters; corporate distributions; organization, reorganization, or liquidation; tax on accumulated earnings; personal holding examples; and tax research.

title: International Accounting
description: International accounting course is an examination of international accounting standards, worldwide accounting systems, international taxation, and covers topics of international transfer pricing and strategic accounting issues in multinational corporations.

title: Accounting for Non-Profit Organizations
description: This course introduces and examines the characteristics relating to the accounting concepts, procedures, and financial reporting requirements of governmental, medical, educational, religious, and other charitable organizations.

title: Foundations of Art and Design â *
description: A broad introduction to concepts, vocabulary, media, and history of art and principles of design that reveals universal qualities in human aesthetic responses, as well as the special differences that define societies and cultures through examples from the past, the present, and from diverse cultures. Opportunities for experiential learning in workshops in various media are an integral part of the course.

title: Art History: Beginning to Early Modern â *
description: A survey of fine art from pre-history to the Baroque period.

title: Advanced Topics in Accounting
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Accounting. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Drawing â *
description: A study of how the illusions of form, light and space are created on a two-dimensional plane, with emphasis on  perception, compositional structure and technique.  This course will analyze drawings and relevant artists to develop an understanding of their historical and cultural contexts and influences.

title: Art History: Early Modern to Present â *
description: A survey of fine art in the Western world from the Baroque period to the present day.

title: Painting â *
description: A study of color theory through color mixing, application, technique and layering. This course will analyze paintings and relevant artists to develop an understanding of their historical and cultural contexts and influences.

title: Accounting II: Introduction to Managerial Accounting
description: Management accounting is the timely generation of financial information in an easily understandable format for use in internal decision making. The course stresses the importance and usefulness of relevant accounting information in short- and long-term planning, cost-control, quality assessment, and decision making.

title: Intermediate Accounting II
description: This course is a continuation of ACC 201. Topics include current and contingent liabilities, long-term debt securities, stockholder's equity, earnings per share, income tax allocation, leases, pensions, accounting changes, statements of cash flow, and financial statement analysis.

title: Cost Accounting I
description: This course is designed and required for the major in accounting. Determination and analysis of the costs of manufacturing products and providing services that attempt to satisfy information needs of management are emphasized. Topics include cost accounting theory and techniques, job order and process costing systems, standard costing, and flexible and master budgeting. The focus is on developing the analytical and quantitative skills that aid in the process of interpreting cost information and making decisions.

title: Ceramics â
description: Clay work involving hand-building and basic glazing techniques for functional and non-functional objects.  This course will analyze ceramic work and relevant artists to develop an understanding of their historical and cultural contexts and influences.

title: Printmaking â
description: The study of printing images from blocks of wood, linoleum, plexiglass, and other techniques for their expressive and aesthetic potential.  This course will analyze prints and relevant artists to develop an understanding of their historical and cultural contexts and influences.

title: Mixed Media â
description: This course will study both content and form through the interaction of multiple media, whether two-dimensional or three-dimensional.  Experimentation with manipulating surfaces, collage, layering, and incorporating techniques such as drawing, painting, photography and design. Investigation of artists and how ideas manifest and take physical form in contemporary art.

title: Advanced Studio
description: In-depth and advanced study of form, conventions, and/or movements of a particular genre such as drawing, painting, or sculpture.  The course may be repeated for credit to allow in-depth focus on different studio genres.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Digital Photography â
description: A study in the creation of images, lighting, and design through the formal system identified as the elements and principles of art. Students will focus on digital capture of images and interpretation through a technical, conceptual and aesthetic approach. Emphasis will be placed on the evolution, history and culture of photography and its social significance and impact on the wider world. Students are to supply their own digital camera, batteries, memory cards and any necessary hardware accessories or software accessories to make the camera functional.

title: Sculpture â
description: A study of form and space relationships through three-dimensional design.  The basic tool, material and technique will be  presented.  This course will analyze sculptures and relevant artists to develop an understanding of their historical and cultural contexts and influences

title: Introductory Topics in Art â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Art. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Field Period in Art and Design
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to apply his/her academic knowledge to a real-career setting.

title: Advanced Topics in Art â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Art. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Introduction to Interpreting
description: This course provides a scope of the interpreting profession and its roles, responsibilities, and ethics within the interpreting profession. Language registers, interpreting models and processes, and interpreting practices will be explored and examined.  Guidelines, protocols, practices, and trends are discussed in interpreting settings in accordance to the national, professional, and international associations.

title: Introductory Topics in Accounting
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Accounting. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Introductory Topics in ASL-English Interpreting
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within ASL-English Interpreting. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: English and ASL Translation
description: This course examines translations in applied linguistics of English and ASL. Challenges encountered in the translation process between ASL and English will be identified, as well as strategies to manage challenges when translating. Analysis of forms and meanings within English and ASL translations will be emphasized. Students will focus on the development of ASL to English translation skills.

title: Consecutive Interpreting
description: This course introduces students to the process and practice of interpretation between ASL and English. Class discussion and activities focus on creating a culturally and linguistically dynamic interpretation while broadening students’ understanding of interpreting demands.

title: Interpreting Specialized Settings
description: An exploration of sight translation, consecutive interpreting, and simultaneous interpreting between ASL and English within medical, educational, legal, social services, and employment contexts. Practical interpreting simulations on register variation and technical vocabulary will be introduced and practiced.

title: Simultaneous Interpreting and Research
description: In this course students continue to practice and refine their ASL and English interpretation skills in a simultaneous format. Linguistic and cultural adaptations are emphasized for  accuracy of interpretations to preserve meaning.

title: Field Period in Art and Design
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to apply his/her academic knowledge to a real-career setting.

title: Interpreting Internship
description: A semester-long, off-campus practical experience in approved settings where students interpret under the guidance and supervision of certified interpreter mentors verified by appropriate professional organizations.

title: Deaf Community Field Period
description: An off-campus education experience with exposure to an organization or long-term event that includes deaf people both as organizers and as consumers.  The goals for the student should include areas of improved language fluency, cultural awareness, and cooperation in serving the goals of the deaf community.  This field period must be evaluated by an ASL or AEI faculty member.

title: Advanced Topics in ASL-English Interpreting
description: An advanced study of ASL-English Interpreting. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: American Sign Language I â
description: This course introduces the basics of American Sign Language (ASL) by understanding and applying foundations of linguistics and is designed for students who have little or no previous knowledge of ASL. Emphasis is upon acquisition of comprehension, production and interactive skills using basic grammatical features. ASL will be taught within contexts and related to general surroundings and everyday life experiences.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: American Sign Language II â
description: This course is designed to continue the development of American Sign Language initiated in ASL 111.  ASL 112 emphasizes the development and refinement of comprehension, production, and interpersonal skills. Additional information about the Deaf community and Deaf culture will be included.

title: DeafBlind Translation
description: This course explores practical considerations when working with DeafBlind consumers. Topics include linguistic and cultural adaptations for tactile interpreting. Applications include translations and tactile projections.

title: Cognitive Processing Skills
description: An introduction to the cognitive processing skills which are components of the complex interpreting process.  Students perform exercises and activities that help to develop the ability to translate and consecutively interpret texts in both ASL and English.  Students are first introduced to individual processing skills that are then combined into the complex act of interpretation. Major skill areas developed include the following: visualization, shadowing, listening and comprehension, abstracting, structuring, paraphrasing, dual task, cloze and sight translation.

title: Lab: American Sign Language II
description: Students practice ASL and work toward fluency through guided and structured experiences including practicing dialogues and conversations under faculty supervision.  Self–reflective reports and weekly participation are a requirement.

title: Introduction to the Deaf Community â
description: An analysis of socio-cultural, linguistic, and educational perspectives of American Sign Language and the Deaf community including its culture, arts, history and people. This course is designed to prepare students take ASL 420 -Deaf Culture. This course is taught in ASL.

title: American Sign Language III â
description: This course builds on the foundation of skills and knowledge learned in ASL 111 and 112. This course continues to expand upon vocabulary, the structure of complex grammatical features in narrative and dialogue formats, along with interactional and conversational skills.

title: Fingerspelling and Numbers in ASL â
description: This course is intended for students who need focused practice to improve both comprehension and production skills in fingerspelling and numbers used within ASL conversational contexts at the intermediate and advanced levels. Receptive skills focus on whole word phrase recognition and fingerspelling/number comprehension in context. Expressive skills focus on the development of speed, clarity, and fluency. The course includes review of rules governing use of fingerspelling within ASL and the use of fingerspelling to support and reinforce English vocabulary recognition within educational contexts.

title: Linguistics of American Sign Language â
description: Analysis of the symbolic and linguistic structure of American Sign Language and other sign systems. Includes aspects of phonology, morphology, syntax, semantics, grammar, and discourse. Examination of bilingual and bicultural practices, sociolinguistics, and related research. Assessment: written examination.  Course will be taught in ASL.

title: American Sign Language VI â
description: As the sixth and final course in a sequence of American Sign Language development, this course is an in-depth study of ASL and designed to increase ASL expressive competence. Students will continue to learn ASL skills such as storytelling, health/ailments, conceptual awareness, ASL grammar analysis, use of classifiers and complex uses of space from video and written texts.

title: ASL/Deaf Literature â
description: This course involves the use of ASL poetry and storytelling. ASL poetry will be analyzed and incorporated as part of receptive/expressive language development and advanced skills enhancement. Includes story-telling techniques through the use of standard American folk lore and other culturally valued forms of literature (ABC stories, one-deaf stories, and sign play). Students will evaluate existing video tapes of deaf poets and sign language stories. Assessment: written examination and demonstration and/or videotaping of storytelling techniques. Course will be taught in ASL.

title: American Sign Language IV â
description: This course is a continuation of ASL 211Â and further builds the development of comprehension, sign production, and discourse skills with anÂ emphasis on complex narration, dialogue, andÂ stories in ASL. Discussions of DeafÂ culture will be included.

title: American Sign Language Field Period
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to improve his/her proficiency in signing ASL, expand ASL vocabulary, acquire knowledge of ASL colloquialisms, learn non-verbal communication skills and advance an understanding of deaf culture. The focus of this field period should be on language acquisition and improvement, not on a career.

title: ASL Discourse Analysis â
description: An analysis of discourse in dialogic genres of English and American Sign Language (ASL) so that interpreting students become explicitly aware of the features of language use in everyday life. Students transcribe and analyze interaction and discourse features of conversations, explanations, interviews, discussions, and other types of dialogue genres while reading and discussing theoretical notions underlying language use. At the conclusion of this course, students will take the ASLPI which will determine mastery of ASL fluency by an outside evaluation agency.  This is a mandatory requirement for students in the ASL-English Interpreting Program. A score of 2 or higher is required to proceed in the AEI program. Students in the ASL program are strongly encouraged to take this evaluation for feedback on their language fluency.

title: Introductory Topics in American Sign Language â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within American Sign Language. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: American Sign Language V â
description: This course applies knowledge and skills of AmericanÂ Sign Language in complex narrative structures. Classifiers are emphasized in narrations.Â Analysis of Deaf cultural values and attitudes of the Deaf community are included.

title: American Sign Language Field Period
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to enhance proficiency in ASL, expand ASL vocabulary, employ ASL grammar, and deepen his/her understanding of Deaf culture through direct engagement with members of the Deaf community. Students will develop a life learning plan that incorporates continued involvement with the Deaf community beyond graduation. The focus of this field period is to increase knowledge of ASL and Deaf culture through experience with the Deaf on-site.

title: Advanced Study in American Sign Language â
description: An advanced study of American Sign Language. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Lab: American Sign Language I
description: Students practice ASL and work toward fluency through guided and structured experiences including practicing dialogues and conversations under faculty supervision.  Self–reflective reports and weekly participation are a requirement.

title: General Biology II â
description: An examination of the diversity of living organisms, the interaction of organisms and the physical environment, evolutionary processes thought to have shaped the continuity and change of organism form throughout time; lecture and laboratory. Course intended for natural science majors.

title: General Biology I â *
description: An examination of the cellular basis of life and how eukaryotic cells interact in the structure and function of multicellular organisms (selected plants and animals); lecture and laboratory. Course intended for natural science and occupational science majors.

title: Human Biology â *
description: Fundamentals of cell biology, developmental biology, and genetics provide a basis for survey of normal structure, function, and interaction of human organ systems. A variety of modern health issues, such as nutrition, cancer, alcoholism, heart disease, and aging, are explored. Course intended for non-science and non-nursing majors.

title: Lab: General Biology II
description: Lab for BIO 136.

title: Lab: Human Biology
description: Lab for BIO 202

title: Lab: Anatomy and Physiology II
description: Lab for BIO 204.

title: Lab: Anatomy and Physiology I
description: Lab for BIO 203.

title: Microbiology â
description: This course examines bacteria and other microorganisms and their importance; aseptic techniques, methods of staining and isolation of bacteria, and various aspects of microbial physiology and control; lecture and laboratory.

title: Deaf Culture â
description: This course is an intermediate-high level course, which provides a deeper perspective on Deaf people who use ASL and their culture. This course also analyzes the history of the Deaf community and Deaf culture in the historical terms of colonialism, as well as anthropological terms of language, culture, traditions, education, arts, social rules, and values. The course will be taught in ASL.

title: Lab: Microbiology
description: Lab for BIO-207

title: Introduction to the Natural Sciences â
description: This course emphasizes the scientific process: the study of the natural world, generation, modification, and dissemination of scientific knowledge, and the application of knowledge through technology. The biological concepts of metabolism, reproduction and development, genetics and evolution are explored, as are the laws and theories that enable us to understand the nature and interrelationship of matter and energy both on the microscopic and macroscopic scale. Relationships and common themes among mathematics, science and technology will be stressed. Historical and modern examples are included to challenge students to comprehend, analyze and apply how scientists do what they do.

title: Introductory Topics in Biology â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Biology. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Lab: General Biology I
description: Lab for BIO 135

title: Lab: Introductory Topics in Biology
description: Lab for BIO-295 .

title: Animal Diversity â
description: A broad spectrum of animals, both invertebrate and vertebrate, will be examined to provide the student with a working knowledge of the modern fauna and the evolutionary mechanisms that have shaped animal structure and function over millions of years.

title: Anatomy and Physiology I â
description: The course begins with cellular chemistry and function, tissues and continues through the nervous, skeletal, and muscular systems.  Homeostasis and structural and functional relationships will be emphasized.

title: Lab: Plant Biology
description: Lab for BIO 301.

title: Genetics â
description: Animals, plants, and microorganisms are used to illustrate the principles of heredity, including a consideration of classical Mendelian transmission genetics, molecular genetics, and population genetics. Students conduct a limited research project and a seminar on a specific topic. Problem-solving approach is emphasized.

title: Lab: Animal Diversity
description: Lab for BIO 305.

title: Lab: Genetics
description: Lab for BIO 318

title: Lab: Ecology
description: Lab for BIO 337.

title: Neuroanatomy and Physiology â
description: Detailed study of the development, morphology, internal configuration, function, and pathological defects of the central nervous system.

title: Anatomy and Physiology II â
description: A continuation of BIO-203 .  Emphasizes homeostasis and structural and functional relationships in the study of cardiovascular, respiratory, urinary, digestive, endocrine, and reproductive systems.

title: Lab: Limnology
description: Lab for BIO 360.

title: Lab: Neuroanatomy and Physiology
description: Lab for BIO 343.

title: Cell and Molecular Biology â
description: The function of Eukaryotic cells is balanced with the study of cellular function at the molecular level with emphasis on replication, transcription and translation. Students also develop an appreciation for molecular techniques and current literature.

title: Limnology â
description: An interdisciplinary study of inland waters: lakes, streams, rivers, estuaries and wetlands. Water topics to be studied include: unique characteristics, distribution, origins, and the biological, physical and chemical composition. A primary focus will be on the ecological and economical importance of water and human impacts. Laboratory and field work are required.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Opportunity for students to conduct investigative study in the laboratory and/or field or to perform some type of creative work not covered by other courses. The student will work with a faculty member to develop a research proposal, and carry out the proposed work. Completed Application for Research and Research Proposal forms are required. The course will culminate with the preparation of a research report. May be repeated.

title: Seminar in Scientific Communication â
description: Instruction in critical reading and analysis of recent primary and secondary source journal articles in the scientific literature. Students will develop and present literature critiques of research articles relevant to their discipline in biology, biochemistry, or environmental science.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Plant Biology â
description: This course will cover various aspects of plant biology, including plant cells, plant anatomy and architecture, physiology and function, ecology, and the roles of plants in the environment, agriculture and society. Laboratory exercises build on lecture themes and provide hands-on learning experiences including field trips.

title: Evolution â
description: This course uses animal and plant examples to illustrate and clarify fundamental concepts of evolution, with emphasis on evolutionary mechanisms and theory. Classical and modern contributions to our understanding of evolution as the unifying concept of biology will be examined.

title: Lab: Modern Laboratory Techniques
description: Lab for BIO-401 .

title: Introduction to Business and Society
description: An examination of contemporary business institutions and their relationship to society. Topics include historical antecedents, basis of the American enterprise system, functional areas of business, and principles of management.

title: Advanced Topics in Biology â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Biology. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Lab: Advanced Topics in Biology
description: Lab for BIO-495 .

title: Modern Laboratory Techniques â
description: A laboratory-based methods and skills course emphasizing molecular biology and biochemistry techniques such as nucleic acid isolation and analysis, recombinant DNA cloning, primer design, endpoint and real time PCR and the use of bioinformatics tools.

title: Immunology â
description: An introductory course in immunology in which the basic principles and concepts are stressed. Topics to be studied include the nature of antigens, haptens, immunoglobulins, and complement and their interactions; the structure, behavior, and interactions of the cell types involved in the immune response; natural resistance and acquired immunity; immune tolerance; allergies; autoimmunity; immune deficiencies; and the histocompatibility complex.

title: Ecology â
description: A qualitative and quantitative study of the interrelationships of organisms and the environment; lecture and laboratory.

title: Introduction to Computer Science
description: We live in a digital world, and to shape it, you need to understand the systems that power it. This course teaches you not just how computers work, but how they have–and will continue to—evolve. You'll get a taste for coding by building your own website, design AI-powered products, learn how to break down technical problems, and become a wiser digital citizen in the process.

title: Senior Research â
description: Opportunity for senior students to conduct original investigative study in the laboratory and/or field, or to perform creative work of the type not covered by other courses. The student will work with a faculty member to develop a research proposal, and carry out the proposed work. Completed Application for Research and Research Proposal forms are required. The work may be a continuation of a project started in BIO/CHM/ENV 398 or another course.  The project will culminate in the preparation of a formal research report. May be repeated.

title: Business Law
description: An introduction to legal principles and their relationships to business organizations. Representative topics include the constitutional authority to regulate business, consumer law, employment and labor relations law, torts and crimes related to business, and intellectual property. A substantial part of the course covers contract law and commercial paper. Cases from New York law are used extensively to assist the students in understanding the everyday applications of the law.

title: Programming for Everyone I
description: Programming is not just about coding; it's about solving problems and thinking logically. This course is a general introduction to coding in Python, one of the most popular and powerful programming languages. Step-by-step, you'll use functions, logic, loops, and libraries to take your ideas from concept to reality. By the end, you’ll be able to create useful applications from scratch, but more importantly solve technical problems - a key skill for everyone.

title: Business Ethics â
description: Foundations and development of business institutions in America; goals, values, and ethics in a business world; ethical dilemmas, ambiguities, conflicts, and uncertainties in business; scope and responsibilities to society, the firm, and ethical principles.

title: Field Period in Business
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the students to apply his/her academic knowledge to a real-career business setting.

title: Advanced Business Law
description: This course explores, among other things, bankruptcy, agency, real and personal property, partnerships, corporations, sales, secured transactions, legal liability of accountants, and the Securities Acts of 1933 and 1934. Cases from New York law are used extensively to assist the student in understanding everyday applications of the law. This course is required for, but not restricted to, accounting majors and provides preparation, in part, for the Uniform Certified Public Accounting Examination (Law Part).

title: Operations and Production Management
description: An in-depth study of operations and production management, including both theory and current practice. Key topics include operations strategies, product and process design, plant capacity and location, manufacturing technology, quality management, continuous improvement, services operations, project management, supply chain management, and JIT/lean manufacturing. Emphasis is placed on general concepts, current practice, and problem solving.

title: Introductory Topics in Business
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Business. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Managerial Marketing
description: Through assigned readings, stimulations, case studies, and class discussions, adult learners are exposed to basic marketing theory and terminology. They are given the opportunity to apply this newly acquired knowledge to analyze real-world cases exploring domestic and international marketing opportunities and problems. This exploration provides adult learners with the skills and confidence to identify and evaluate critical marketing data. Adult learners learn to use data to develop workable and successful programs to solve problems and capitalize on opportunities.

title: International Business
description: This course offers an introduction to the vital issues of international business including economics, political, cultural and operational differences with emphasis on multinational corporate policy and strategy.

title: Senior Seminar in Natural Science â
description: This is considered the capstone course for students completing majors within the natural sciences. The course focuses on instruction in critical thinking, analysis and information literacy in the science field and current topics in modern science. Each student will write a substantial research paper on a scientific area of interest using knowledge from previous coursework. Each student will complete a public presentation of his/her topic.

title: Entrepreneurship
description: This course unites business theory and practical application by means of completing a hands-on microenterprise project. This project allows students to practice entrepreneurship by managing their own for-profit business venture. The students, with supervision, will perform the functions of management, marketing, and accounting. Student entrepreneurs will be responsible for product development, procurement and supply, quality control, labeling, packaging, advertising and sales, ordering and distribution, and financial reporting for the project.

title: Application Development II: Frontend
description: This course will have you building sleek, dynamic web apps using tools, such as React and Angular. Learn to create stunning front-end interfaces, seamlessly connect them with backend services, and ensure everything runs smoothly. You'll dive into component-based architecture, manage state like a pro, and master the art of making your apps fast and responsive. By the end, you'll be ready to create full-stack applications that stand out and wow users.

title: Application Development I: Backend
description: This course is all about equipping you with the skills to build dynamic, database-driven web applications—the core of great app development. You'll create powerful backend scripts using Java and JavaScript, build RESTful APIs, and develop middleware for user authentication and session management. By the end, you'll create apps that are not only functional but also beautiful, engaging, and solve real-world problems.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Lifespan Human Development â
description: Human development from conception to old age; physical growth, perception, cognition, emotional growth, social interaction, and personality.

title: Advanced Topics in Business
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Business. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Senior Seminar in Sustainability â
description: This course will serve as a upper level seminar that discusses and analyzes selected topics in the social, environmental, and economic aspects of sustainability. The course will include a student initiated research project including selection of research area, literature review, generation of hypotheses, variable identification, data-gathering analysis, and presentation and discussion of results. Current issues in sustainability and guest speakers are the foundation for class discussion and problem-driven inquiry.

title: Business Law
description: This module examines the history, background, sources and influences of our modern day law as it pertains to the business activities of individuals, corporations and other legal entities, with particular emphasis upon business litigation, legal liabilities, alternative dispute resolution techniques, and the laws governing contracts, creditors' rights, secured transactions, bankruptcy, agency, partnerships, and corporations.

title: Web Development
description: Have you ever wondered how you’d manage without websites—no online banking, instant information, or social media? Fortunately, web development brings these essential services to life. In this course, you'll learn the fundamentals of web development, from HTML and CSS to JavaScript. You'll discover how to create visually appealing and interactive sites. By the end, you'll build responsive, user-friendly websites that captivate and engage users.

title: Programming for Everyone II
description: This course teaches you to build data-powered software products.  Building on fundamentals of Python programming, you'll learn how to build software that interacts with other software, stays up-to-date, and is something that people want to use and build on top of. In doing so, you'll master all the skills necessary to pursue advanced programming and app development.

title: Development in Middle Childhood â
description: Development from age 6 to puberty; theories of child development; social, emotional, personality, and perceptual-intellectual development; family, peer, and social influences in development; and sex differences and achievement behavior.

title: Introductory Topics in Child and Family Studies â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Child and Family Studies. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Infancy and Early Childhood Development â
description: Development from conception through preschool years; physical development, intellectual functioning, achievement behavior, social and personality development, sex differences, and application of developmental principles; theories of child development, including Piaget's concepts of intellectual functioning.

title: Adolescence â
description: Biological, societal, familial, and peer influences in adolescent behavior; problems of youth in society.

title: Intermediate Topics in Child and Family Studies â
description: An intermediate study of a specific topic within sociology. Intended for sophomores and junior students. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Family Interaction â
description: This course examines the interactional processes of family systems including communication processes, bonding behaviors, management of conflict and aggression, and negotiation of family crisis.  We will be applying three perspectives (structural, intergenerational, and contextual models) to our exploration of family functioning.

title: Sociology of the Family â
description: Structure, process and function of family systems, emphasizing historical development and change in such areas as economics, gender roles and socialization; types of families, including extended, nuclear, single parent, blended, binuclear, and gay and lesbian families, noting variation in family form and impact of social policies.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Lab: Elements of Physiological Chemistry
description: Lab for CHM-107

title: Advanced Topics in Child and Family Studies â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Child and Family Studies.  Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject.  The student may repeat the course but not the topic.

title: Elements of Physiological Chemistry â *
description: An introduction to the fundamental concepts of chemistry that are necessary to understand key principles relevant to the human body's basic biochemicals and their activities.  This course is intended for students in health related fields.  Topics include the composition of atoms and molecules, mass balance, energy, properties of gasses and solutions, acid base chemistry, nuclear chemistry, the chemistry of carbon, and the major classes of biological molecules.  Concepts will be reinforced in the laboratory.

title: General Chemistry I â *
description: Introduction to fundamental chemical principles and concepts. Emphasis on atomic and molecular structure, basic bonding theory, and reactions in an aqueous environment. Strong laboratory component. Lecture and laboratory.

title: General Chemistry II â
description: This is the second course of the two-course sequence in general chemistry. It covers advanced bonding concepts, chemical kinetics, and thermodynamics. Solution equilibria, solubility and complex ion equilibria, and electrochemistry also are important components of the course. Laboratory work stresses quantitative analysis using traditional gravimetric and volumetric techniques, as well as instrumental methods.

title: Strategic Management
description: This course introduces the student to the process by which strategic business decisions are made and implemented. It also incorporates prior functional course material in an integrated manner as a foundation for successful business management. Formal studies, casework, and a comprehensive term project are used to reinforce learning. This is the capstone course for all majors with the Division of Business and Management.

title: Organic Chemistry I â
description: Principles of covalent bonding as they apply to aliphatic and aromatic systems, including Lewis structures, molecular geometry, and bond polarity. Analytical techniques such as ultraviolet and infrared spectroscopy are used to investigate structure. Major emphasis is placed on the knowledge of nomenclature and functional group chemistry. An introduction to reaction mechanisms and stereochemistry is provided. Laboratory work stresses functional groups, separation techniques, and qualitative analysis. Lecture and laboratory.

title: Lab: General Chemistry II
description: Lab for CHM 112.

title: Organic Chemistry II â
description: Synthesis and analysis of organic compounds.  Nuclear magnetic resonance and mass spectroscopy are used as analysis tools in the identification of unknowns. Insights into electrophilic and nucleophilic reaction mechanisms. Laboratory emphasizes synthetic schemes and qualitative organic analysis.

title: Lab: Organic Chemistry II
description: Lab for CHM 207.

title: Lab: General Biochemistry
description: Lab for CHM-310L

title: Introductory Topics in Chemistry â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Chemistry. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: General Biochemistry â
description: A survey of the structures and functions of the major groups of biologically important macromolecules (proteins, nucleic acids, lipids and carbohydrates), as well as their metabolism and regulation. The laboratory provides experience with common experimental procedures and emphasizes techniques for protein and DNA analysis.

title: Field Period in Child and Family Studies
description: A supervised practicum in sites providing services to children and families.  Working individually with faculty, students will identify specific learning goals and research, and literature that will inform their experience and that will provide the basis for a summative analytical report integrating research and practice. Students must submit an initial proposal for approval by the end of the fourth week of the semester prior to the anticipated Field Period. May be repeated for credit.

title: Lab: Introductory Topics in Chemistry
description: Lab for CHM 295

title: Analytical Chemistry â
description: Fundamentals of quantitative chemical analysis. Gravimetric, volumetric, and spectroscopic determinations relevant to acid-base, complexometric, potentiometric equilibria. Lecture stresses the theory of multiple equilibria. Laboratory stresses hands-on, multiple determinations. The analytical chemistry of environmentally- and forensically-significant problems forms a major part of the course. The understanding and proper use of descriptive statistics is stressed throughout the course.

title: Lab: General Chemistry I
description: Lab for CHM 111.

title: Lab for Environmental Chemistry
description: Lab for CHM 340

title: Introduction to Physical Chemistry â
description: Theory and application of the laws of thermodynamics: heat, work, energy, enthalpy, entropy, free energy. Properties of solutions, chemical equilibrium, kinetics, and quantum chemistry.

title: Environmental Chemistry â
description: Study of the influence of chemical interactions internal and external to ecosystems and on a global level -- reactions, solubilizations, equilibrium and dynamics in and between soils, freshwater, oceans, and the earth's atmosphere. Influences of anthropogenic perturbations to the hydrosphere, lithosphere, and atmosphere.

title: Inorganic Chemistry â
description: This course will present a survey of the chemistry of the elements and their compounds, with a focus on atomic and molecular structure, periodicity, bonding, and chemical reactivity. Topics will include bonding models, molecular symmetry and structure, reactivity, and properties of transition metal complexes. Applications in the areas of bioinorganic, solid-state, and organometallic chemistry will be discussed.

title: Lab: Organic Chemistry I
description: Lab for CHM 206.

title: Senior Seminar in Natural Science â
description: This is considered the capstone course for students completing majors within the natural sciences. The course focuses on instruction in critical thinking, analysis and information literacy in the science field and current topics in modern science. Each student will write a substantial research paper on a scientific area of interest using knowledge from previous coursework. Each student will complete a public presentation of his/her topic.

title: Seminar in Scientific Communication â
description: Instruction in critical reading and analysis of recent primary and secondary source journal articles in the scientific literature. Students will develop and present literature critiques of research articles relevant to their discipline in biology, biochemistry, or environmental science.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Opportunity for students to conduct investigative study in the laboratory and/or field or to  perform some type of creative work not covered by other courses. The student will work with a faculty member to develop a research proposal, and carry out the proposed work. Completed Application for Research and Research Proposal forms are required. The course will culminate with the preparation of a research report. May be repeated.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Advanced Topics in Chemistry â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Chemistry. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Senior Research â
description: Opportunity for senior students to conduct original investigative study in the laboratory and/or field, or to perform creative work of the type not covered by other courses. The student will work with a faculty member to develop a research proposal, and carry out the proposed work. Completed Application for Research and Research Proposal forms are required. The work may be a continuation of a project started in BIO/CHM/ENV 398 or another course.  The project will culminate in the preparation of a formal research report. May be repeated.

title: Health Services Introductory Seminar
description: This seminar course introduces Community Health and Wellness (Health Services) students to the major.  Students will engage in learning activities to promote success in the program by familiarizing themselves with career options related to the major.  The course will include a broad overview of current issues related to healthcare in the United States and beyond.

title: Introduction to Health Care Systems
description: The course will enable students to receive initial exposure to health care systems and attitudes applicable to the health care industry.  The concepts of health, wellness, and preventative care are evaluated as well as ethical and legal responsibilities of today's health care provider.  More specifically, this course introduces and describes the U.S. health care system, addressing its components and complexities.  The historical basis for the system's organization will be discussed, as well as the various modes of health care delivery and the ways health care is organized, financed and delivered in the United States.

title: Advanced Biochemistry â
description: Provides an in-depth examination of selected areas in biochemistry with particular emphasis on topics of current interest.

title: Lab: Introduction to Physical Chemistry
description: Lab for CHM 328.

title: Orientation to Professional Practice II
description: This interdisciplinary seminar course provides an overview of the foundational concepts and principles of health and human services. It aims to introduce students to the fundamental knowledge, skills, and practice benefits of an interdisciplinary approach to care. By integrating various perspectives, students will gain an introductory understanding of the roles, responsibilities, and collaborative nature of the professions in promoting health, well-being, and functional independence.

title: Introduction to Pathophysiology for Community Health
description: This course introduces students to an overview of the disordered physiological processes associated with disease and/or injury that can occur in the human body. This course will highlight the underlying cause and effect of health, wellness, and illness in individuals and populations and prepare students to effectively communicate with community health practitioners and medical professionals.

title: Research Methods for Health Sciences â
description: This course introduces the concepts and principles of the research process in the health sciences that will lay the foundation for the understanding, appreciation and application of research in health care. Students will become familiar with research terminology and develop an understanding of methods of inquiry including the development of a question. Topics will include ethical considerations when conducting research, research designs, data collection and analysis, and the processes and procedures for conducting research. Students will be able to critically read and evaluate existing research, identify the strengths and weaknesses of different methodological approaches, and enhance their ability to think and reason critically in order to make ethical decisions involving research.

title: Medical Terminology/Pharmacology
description: This course provides an overview of medical terminology and pharmacology. Students will learn the appropriate medical terminology for symptoms, diseases, treatments, and medical tests, enabling them to communicate effectively with interprofessional teams. Students will develop an understanding of medications and their impact on the body systems. The course covers the basic principles of pharmacology, including pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, medication development, the FDA approval process, and interaction effects of medication.

title: Introductory Topics in Community Health and Wellness
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Community Health and Wellness. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Theoretical Principles in Health Behavior
description: The purpose of the course is to provide the student with an understanding of the application of the social and behavioral sciences to health.  The course reviews behavioral, social and cultural factors related to individual and population health and health disparities over the life course.  Public health issues related to individuals and populations will be examined in terms of social and behavioral theories, empirical research findings, and methodologies.

title: Lab-Clinical Neuroscience for Health Professionals
description: Lab for CHW-350

title: Clinical Neuroscience for Health Professionals
description: Clinical Neuroscience is a comprehensive introductory course for Occupational Therapy (OT) and Health Science (HS) students with an orientation towards the scientific and medical foundations underlying the basic neurosciences, and the clinical conditions and approaches encountered by OT and HS practitioners.  Basic neuroanatomy and neurophysiology will be emphasized, and will be accompanied by extensive discussion of clinical applications and neurological conditions that the practitioners will encounter in patients entrusted to their therapeutic care.

title: Health Promotion and Prevention Policy
description: This course focuses on the development of advocacy and leadership at the local, state, national, and international level.  Students will study topics including ethics, policy making and legislation/regulation of community health and wellness concerns at all levels.

title: Epidemiology and Public Health
description: This course introduces the basic principles and methods of epidemiology, with an emphasis on critical thinking, analytic skills, and application to health policy.  Topics include outcome measures, methods of adjustment, surveillance, quantitative study designs, and sources of data.  Students will practice using epidemiology to better understand, characterize and promote health at a population level through experiential learning activities.

title: Capstone II
description: Students will work in small project teams with faculty advisement to complete a scholarly, culminating capstone project.  The faculty-led small group project allows student(s) to demonstrate application of knowledge gained. The baccalaureate capstone is an integral part of the major’s curriculum design and is completed over the course of two semesters.

title: Advanced Topics in Community Health and Wellness
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Community Health and Wellness. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Editing and Publishing Practicum
description: Practical application of basic publishing and editing skills with a focus on creative venues (literary and arts journals). Course focuses on soliciting and gathering materials for publication, making editorial judgments, and preparing periodicals for traditional and online publication. Students engage in conversations with journal editors, participate in workshops, and gain experiential learning through production and online publication of Keuka College’s arts journal, Red Jacket. Students may also be encouraged to contribute their work to Red Jacket.

title: Lab: Advanced Topics in Chemistry
description: Lab for CHM 495

title: Public Speaking â
description: Theory and practice of speaking before a variety of audiences. Research for and preparation of original speeches.

title: Group Culture and Communication â
description: This course explores how formal and informal groups and organizations’ rules and values (or cultures) are created, interpreted, sustained, and replicated through a vast network of human verbal and nonverbal communication. This course applies the basic understanding of the communication strategies discussed in Communication and Our World(s) to interpersonal, group, and organizational communication settings, and provides strategies for assessing and improving communication effectiveness.

title: Visual Communication Design â *
description: This course examines the history, theory, and practices of visual communication design. Through integrative methods, students explore the diverse range of specialties that comprise design, from graphic design, to information, interaction, and user experience design. Guided by formative feedback, students cultivate design thinking through active collaboration on individual and group projects.

title: Graphic Design â
description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of graphic design. Through skills-based application, students develop facility with both traditional and digital design tools. In addition to investigating theory, hierarchy, color, typography, layout, and production, students explore strategies for concept development and creative problem solving to shape and convey visual messages.

title: Happiness, Well-Being and Health
description: Concepts and constructs of wellness and health promotion are studied and analyzed in relation to one's self and the public's health.  Students will integrate health behavior theories along with models of community health promotion and wellness with the objectives of Healthy People.  Students will apply wellness and health promotion principles and strategies to one's own life and a proposed target population within the community.

title: Introductory Topics in Communication â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Communication. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Digital Media Integration
description: A project-based course that focuses on the tools, techniques, and theories of digital media production. Students cultivate proficiency with diverse methods of visual, audio, motion, and interactive design. Specific emphasis is placed on software application and the development of digital media as a means of visual communication.

title: Digital Space and Social Change â
description: This course explores the creation and maintenance of digital public spaces by looking at how digital media ignites social movements, agitates popular and political entities, and inspires (or undermines) social change. Students interrogate information use and misuse, practice transdisciplinary research methodologies, and cultivate multimodal composition and digital literacy skills. Provides an introduction to public sphere theory, visual and digital rhetoric.

title: Professional and Technical Writing â
description: The study and practice of professional writing, including but not limited to business and technical communications, formal proposals and presentations, research reports, and grant writing. Emphasis is placed on context-specific writing strategies, basic visual design, and negotiating the rhetorical expectations of professional communication genres.

title: Interactive Design
description: This course provides an overview of interactive design principles for digital interfaces and experiential applications. Through a human-centered design and project-based approach, students explore methods of interactivity from interactive experience design to website development. Projects spotlight processes for further development, including research, sketching, wireframing, prototyping, and user testing.

title: Publication Design â
description: This course offers a complementary blend of history, theory, and the principles of publication design. In addition to providing a foundation in information design, students become familiar with writing content, understanding software applications, developing workflow techniques (including style sheets and layouts), and properly using typography to design documents for print.

title: Advanced Design
description: Students develop proficiency in graphic and visual communication design through advanced study of design theory, systems, and methods. Investigating how design influences public awareness and community engagement, this project-based course challenges students to create designs (both individually and in groups) that promote social good. Students acquire industry preparation and begin to establish effective design portfolios.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Capstone I
description: Students will work in small project teams with faculty advisement to complete a scholarly, culminating capstone project.  The faculty-led small group project allows student(s) to demonstrate application of knowledge gained. The baccalaureate capstone is an integral part of the major’s curriculum design and is completed over the course of two semesters.

title: Communication and Our World(s) â *
description: The messages we send and receive, from spoken word and written statements, to facial expressions, illustrations, mass media, and more, shape how we make meaning of ourselves, our identities, and the world around us. This course provides a survey of communication theories, methods of communication (intra/interpersonal, group, organizational, intercultural, mediated, and other systems), and communication systems.

title: Visual Communication Design Field Period
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to apply his/her academic knowledge to a real-career setting.

title: Senior Capstone: Convergence in Professional Practice â
description: The Professional Capstone course is a concluding interdisciplinary, collaborative environment to practice and prepare for professional success after graduation. This will be done through documentation, reflection and presentation of students’  body of work for audience/client interactions; preparation of professional materials; intensive mentoring; and an interdisciplinary collaborative project with professional focus. Students engage in a team-based interdisciplinary project, often with a client sponsor, representing a commercial, cultural, or social concern.  Students develop strong creative solutions in the context of real world constraints. Guest talks will be frequently included. Emphasis is on teamwork, leadership and client interaction.

title: Advanced Topics in Communication â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Communication. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Introduction to Criminal Justice â
description: Study of the criminal justice system, including law enforcement, the courts, and correctional methods; origins, ideologies, and consequences of various systems, both historically and cross-culturally, with emphasis on contemporary American criminal justice.

title: Relations of Law Enforcement â
description: Issues of law enforcement agencies at local, state, and federal levels; interrelations with other criminal justice agencies and community groups, such as the media and neighborhood watch groups; problem areas, such as (perceived) police intrusion, corruption, and excessive force.

title: Visual Communication Design Field Period
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to apply his/her academic knowledge to a real-career setting.

title: Criminal Justice Ethics â
description: This course explores theories of ethics  and justice in their application to criminal justice professional practice. It takes a sociological approach by exploring choices and issues faced by professionals in case studies drawn from the criminal justice system, relating these to major theories of ethics and justice, social structure, and social interaction. Review of selected research will be used to demonstrate the undesirable outcomes associated with ethical lapses. Students will practice applying theoretical methods to the resolution of moral dilemmas and ethical issues at all levels-from criminal justice policy making to enforcement and corrections. The course will review codes of ethics that establish standards of behavior expected in various fields of criminal justice. Students will come to understand what constitutes a clear and consistent ethical decision as they are encouraged to compare formal ethical systems to their own personal beliefs and to relate both of these to choices they might someday face in the criminal justice professions.

title: Punishment, Treatment and Corrections â
description: Philosophies, theories, and practices in the post-conviction stage of criminal justice; institutionalization and its alternatives, such as parole, community service, probation, and restorative justice.

title: Juvenile Delinquency and Juvenile Justice â
description: Juvenile delinquency is a unique kind of crime, different from ordinary adult crime in etiology, motive, prevention, justice system, and treatment. This course examines the theoretical perspectives in analysis and explanation of delinquency. This course also covers the history of the juvenile justice system, how juveniles become involved in the juvenile justice system, the role of police, probation, DA, judges, schools and the community in the juvenile justice system.

title: Criminology â
description: This course offers a survey and critical  evaluation of criminological theories and criminological research. An emphasis is the evaluation of the theories and research methods as they apply to select types of crimes. Implications for research and the treatment and control of crime are addressed.

title: Victimology â
description: This course involves the study of issues related to crime victimization. An overview of the historical and emerging role of victimology as a field of study provides the foundation necessary to examine patterns of victimization. A survey of theories of victimization emphasizes how the recognition of victims in the study of crime influences criminological theory and research. Issues particular to types of offenses and to specific populations of victims are examined and discussed. Myths surrounding crime victims and victimization are critically evaluated for their accuracy and explanatory value. The relationship between the crime victim and the criminal justice system is also examined. Implications for research and policy are explored.

title: Introduction to Cybercrime
description: Issues facing law enforcement at local, state and federal levels involve the use of computers both to facilitate crime and as the target of crime. Cybercrime is a broad term; this course is meant to provide a brief history of the emergence and extent of cybercrime, responses by law enforcement, legislation, jurisdictional challenges and the social impact.  An overview of the operation of computers, cellular networks, and the Internet will be covered. Various types of cybercrimes, cyber-criminals, and methods involved will be examined.  This course will include case studies involving cybercrime to facilitate understanding and learning objectives for students.

title: White Collar and Corporate Crime â
description: This course examines the conceptualization, operational definitions and typologies of white collar and corporate crime. Methods of studying these crimes are evaluated. A survey of theories and a review of research findings and case studies serves to identify the impacts of these crimes on both the individual and society. They also highlight the individual, organizational, and contextual variables related to offending. This course provides an introduction to the various social, political, and legal controls on white collar and corporate offending, and policy implications for responding to and controlling these crimes.

title: Environmental Sociology â
description: This course is an introduction to the  field of environmental sociology, the study of the interaction between human societies, social behavior and the natural environment.  Focusing on modern societies, we examine social and cultural factors that 1) affect the overall societal-environmental relationship and, 2) affect environmental problems and collective efforts to respond to them.  Social factors include social-structural elements such as the economy, crime, government and communities as well as patterns of social inequality.  Cultural factors include prevailing and competing beliefs about the natural environment and values as manifested in lifestyle, aesthetics and civic participation.  Social movements addressing these factors will also be examined.

title: Criminal Law â
description: Comparative study of criminal law with emphasis on the United States Constitution, highlighting first, fourth, fifth, and sixth amendments; prosecution and defense in crimes against persons, property, and public order; application of New York state penal law and definitions.

title: Criminology/Criminal Justice Field Period
description: Initial educational experience in criminology and criminal justice agency setting to learn basic operations and participate in agency activities.  Involves final group discussion and written analytical report.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Intermediate Topics in Criminology â
description: An intermediate study of a specific topic within  criminal justice. Intended for sophomores and junior students. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Criminology and Criminal Justice Field Period
description: Advanced educational experience in criminology and criminal justice agency setting to learn basic operations and participate in agency activities.  Involves final group discussion and written analytical report.

title: Decision Making for Criminal Justice Administrators
description: Criminal justice leadership helps criminal justice professionals balance legal and ethical considerations in decision-making. This course is designed to enable students to use various decision-making models and techniques for more effective decisions making in criminal justice. Students will be exposed to Statistical Analysis, Preferential Analysis, Decision Trees, as well as other decision- making models used in criminal justice administration. Emphasis will be placed on group and team decision-making.

title: Criminal Investigation â
description: Methods of step-by-step inquiry in search of evidence to establish facts connected with crimes, their commission, and the perpetrators; methods of criminalistics to examine and evaluate physical crime evidence; the role of law in these processes.

title: Courts, Prosecution and Defense â
description: Study of the structures, courtroom work groups; lower criminal courts, trial courts, and appellate courts; judges, prosecutors, and defense counsel; prosecution, adjudication, and sentencing.

title: Leadership in Criminal Justice
description: This course will engage students in a critical examination of the current trends in criminal justice as well as the organizational conditions that create new challenges and requisite skills for criminal justice leaders. In addition to skill development in the areas of goal setting, problem solving, and communication, this course will include assessment of personal leadership style, decision-making, and capacity to act as a change-agent for the criminal justice organization. The ability to lead others will be a central theme of the course.

title: Advanced Topics in Criminology and Criminal Justice â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Criminology. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Introductory Topics in Criminology and Criminal Justice â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Criminology. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Introduction to Dance â
description: An overview of the study of dance as an art form through discussion of dance and its relation to the other arts. Technique sessions introduce students to classical forms and exploration of modern and jazz modes. Emphasis is on the fundamentals of movement, rhythm, composition, and style. This course provides students with an opportunity to experience and develop an appreciation for dance.

title: Dance, Self and Culture â
description: A consideration of the fundamentals of movement, dance composition and personal style as expressed within a range of cultures from around the world.  Students will be introduced to some of the many forms and methods of self-expression through dance from around the globe, and the ways in which different cultures conceive the self and its awareness in and through the body, with the intent that these encounters will inform the student's own experiences and experiments in the art of dance.

title: Movement in the Wider World â
description: An exploration of how movement creates meaning in our everyday lives by examining different dance forms and the evolution of movement in performance and in everyday expression.  Students will learn to recognize and to interpret body movement, develop an increased awareness of mind-body connectivity, and learn how to use movement to enhance personal expression and communication.  The course will culminate in a major-specific project that requires specific analysis of movement within students' major and will therefore enhance studies in Occupational Therapy, Psychology, Business, and Education, for example.

title: Principles of Macroeconomics â
description: National income analysis; aggregate demand and supply of goods and services, aggregate investment and savings, and equilibrium in the national economy. Fiscal policy, money, banking, and monetary policy.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Principles of Microeconomics â *
description: This course will explore price theory and its application to the factors of production, and the concept of elasticity of supply and demand. The course will also cover the relationship of organizations to production, costs, markets, pricing, and alternative economic systems. International trade and monetary policy will also be explained.

title: Introductory Topics in Economics â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic in Economics. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Sustainable Development â
description: This course explores the economic, environmental, and social strategies now employed to achieve sustainable economic development, both in advanced economies and those less developed.

title: Advanced Topics in Economics â
description: An advanced study of a specific  topic in Economics. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Safety in Schools Workshop
