description: This workshop focuses on safety education, covering topics such as alcohol and substance abuse prevention, fire and arson prevention, as mandated by Education Law section 808. It also emphasizes the importance of personal health and safety, nutrition, and the effects of dangerous or abusive environments on students' ability to learn. This workshop fulfills a requirement for New York State teacher certification and is graded on a pass/fail basis, with an "S" for satisfactory completion or a "U" for unsatisfactory.

title: Introductory Topics in Dance â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Dance. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Mandated Reporting Workshop
description: This workshop is mandatory for obtaining New York State teaching certification. It outlines teachers' responsibilities in recognizing and reporting child abuse. The course is graded on a pass/fail basis, with an "S" for satisfactory completion or a "U" for unsatisfactory. Candidates complete the workshop online through the Office of Child and Family Services' website.

title: Violence Prevention and DASA Workshop
description: A workshop addressing violence prevention, bullying intervention, and the creation of safe schools is required for the New York State teacher certification. It provides guidance in accordance with the Safe Schools Against Violence in Education Act (SAVE) and Dignity for All Students (DASA) legislation. The course will address warning signs, statutes, regulations, and policies, effective classroom management techniques, and how to participate in an effective school/community referral process. The course is graded on a pass/fail basis, with an "S" for satisfactory completion or a "U" for unsatisfactory.

title: Introduction to Education â
description: The purpose of this course is to introduce students to important foundations of American education. The focus is on the role of schools in American society, and the diversity of learners in schools today. Legal, historical and special education issues impacting the education of learners in American schools are introduced. The structure and governance of schools, issues related to curriculum, and the profession of teaching will also be explored. The course will also introduce the students to theories and theorists, and the importance of planning for instruction including writing lesson goals and objectives through the creation of learning segments.  The ultimate goal of this course is to help students begin to develop critical and multiple perspectives about education in a democratic society, and the importance of meeting the needs of all learners in this setting.

title: Advanced Topics in Dance â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Dance. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Early Child Perspective in Disabilities â
description: This course focuses on the  developmental, learning, behavioral, and social characteristics and needs of infants, preschoolers, and young children with disabilities.  Course content includes focus upon the historical perspective of special education, the concepts of early intervention, family-centered intervention practices, interagency collaboration and appropriate strategies for the management and instruction of exceptional students in early childhood environments.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Educating Students at Risk â
description: In support of the education of all students, this course reviews the philosophical, historical, theoretical and legal foundations of special education and the education of other categories of students who are considered at-risk.  From this broad perspective, students examine the needs and treatment of students with disabilities as well as other groups including but not limited to migrant, homeless, and incarcerated youth; minorities; gay, lesbian, bi- and transgendered students; first Americans, students with mental health challenges, and students living in poverty.  The course also reviews state and federal legislation that impacts educational practice for these students, including the individuals with Disabilities Education Act, and Elementary and Secondary Education Act, (NCLB), and court cases defining ELL education; related district and school decisions are examined as well.  An overarching goal is to define what it means to be a special educator or an educator of students who are at risk for other reasons.  Issues of social justice and how educators provide a free appropriate public education in the least restrictive environment are explored.

title: Infants and Toddlers
description: This course is a general introduction to the unique world of early childhood education and developmentally appropriate practice. Specifically, this course is an orientation to the issues, challenges, opportunities and curriculum of early childhood education from Birth to 36 months. This course examines the principles and theory behind the planning of developmentally appropriate, effective, safe, and healthy environments for infants and toddlers, with an emphasis on play-based curriculum aligned with the New York State Early Learning Guidelines. The impact of culture, language, abilities and early intervention will be addressed. Partnering with families to provide nurturance, learning experiences, and advocacy will be examined. The roles of the early childhood educator will be analyzed. A 5-hour field experience is included in this course.

title: Exploratory Education Field Period
description: An off-campus experience in a public or private school as an exploration of teaching as an appropriate career choice. It should provide an exposure to the demands of the teaching profession and the nature of school-aged children. This Field Period does not fulfill either of the two required education Field Periods.

title: Assessment in Inclusive Schools
description: In this course designed for Childhood and Adolescent majors, participants will experience, learn about, and create a variety of assessments, including formative and summative. Students will design an array of assessments aligned with NYS standards. Topics will include benchmark assessments for response to intervention and diagnostics for students with IEPs. In addition, students will evaluate a norm-referenced, standardized tests.

title: Early Language and Literacy
description: This course focuses on language and literacy in early childhood, birth through second grade. The emphasis is on supporting learning across this span of development in different naturalistic and instructional settings. To that end, learning theories, development, and appropriate instructional strategies for the acquisition of communicative skills, emergent literacy, and beginning reading and writing are emphasized. The New York State Early Learning Guidelines and New York State Standards for English Language Arts provide the framework for instruction within a balanced, thematic approach. The course also includes a 5-hour field experience in an early childhood setting.

title: Teaching in Inclusive Settings
description: This course emphasizes the role of the special education teacher as decision-maker; one who manages the instructional/learning environment to achieve optimal outcomes for all students. Included in this course are the preparation of IEPs & IFSPs, co-teaching strategies for inclusive settings, communications with students, parents, peers and colleagues, test and instructional modifications, and the use of assistive technology. A distinct feature of this course is an examination of the relationship between the regular and special educators and the need for collaboration to ensure positive learning outcomes for all students B-2.

title: Integrated Methods of Teaching in the Childhood Classroom
description: This course is an investigation into how to teach content in the inclusive elementary classroom, with an emphasis on using the New York State Standards as the framework for instructional planning and assessment. Students will apply a constructivist model of teaching with learner-centered techniques that integrate the content areas,  using hands-on learning, manipulatives, inquiry-based and meaningful learning activities at the appropriate developmental level of students. As a result of this course, students will be able to write learning segments and experiences and make appropriate modifications for a diverse group of learners. Students will be able to develop learning opportunities that incorporate brain-based theory, multiple intelligences theory, and the specific learning styles of students. A variety of teaching strategies will be explored, including the following: use of centers, inquiry and problem-based approaches, direct instruction, the use of technology, and cooperative learning. Ten hours of field experience in elementary classrooms, to identify “theory in practice,” round out the learning in this course.

title: Philosophical Foundations of Education â
description: This course introduces the historical, social, and philosophical foundations of American educational system.  Students will learn about European and other Western philosophies and theories of education affecting the structure of American educational thought.  Continued development of the personal philosophy of education is included.

title: Literacy in Inclusive Schools
description: This course provides an introduction to the methods and materials for instruction in reading, writing, and the language arts in the inclusive elementary classroom. The emphasis will be on the New York State Standards for Language Arts with integration of those materials into thematic units and literature-based unit planning. The integration of skills instruction into an authentic balanced language program is fundamental to this course. Course readings and activities will guide students as they develop an understanding and repertoire of instructional techniques for working with children of diverse learning styles and developmental levels.

title: Language in Use â
description: The roots of this course are in sociolinguistics and the application of linguistics. Students learn about language as social experience - as a situated, fluid communicative phenomenon. Following an introduction to the defining nature and structural components of language, the course briefly investigates language development before focusing on how language is used in different contexts, ranging from more formal settings such as classrooms and places of work to less formal, more personal social settings. Students examine the demands and expectations of successful language use in these various contexts based on such concepts as academic language and language registers. Language variety and identity are explored as well related to regional dialect, ethnicity, race, and gender. This course also touches on trends in language use associated with globalization, such as the spread of English, multilingualism, code switching, and translanguaging and the impact of these on education. The overarching goal of this course is for students to learn how individuals effectively make meaning with one another across a variety of situations and contexts.

title: Early Childhood Literacy and the Diverse Learner
description: This course provides a continued investigation of learning and instruction in language and literacy (reading, writing, listening and speaking) for the early childhood educator.  Using the New York State Early Learning Guidelines and the Common Core English Language Arts standards, the course emphasizes how to provide individualized instruction in literacy for English Language Learners, students who are gifted, those with disabilities, and others who may need this type of instruction to achieve.  Instruction is provided in how to use literacy assessments to determine appropriate goals for these students and in how to determine and implement corresponding instructional strategies.  Culturally relevant pedagogy and other strategies that take students' background into account as part of learning are discussed as well.  Interaction with consultant teachers, other specialists, and parents will be addressed.

title: Childhood Literacy and the Diverse Learner
description: This course continues the investigation of methods and materials for teaching and assessing literacy/ELA skills of elementary-aged children. Aligned with the NYS Standards for Language Arts, it focuses on tailoring instruction to meet the diverse needs of students, including those who are advanced learners and those who face challenges in developing literacy skills. Students will explore key theories and research on first and second language acquisition, equipping them to address legal, linguistic, and community-related considerations when working with students who are learning English. The course also covers Science of Reading, and strategies for collaborating with consultant teachers, specialists, and parents to support student success.

title: Adolescent Integrative Methods of Teaching in the Classroom
description: This course is an investigation into how to teach content in the inclusive adolescent classroom, with an emphasis on using the current NYS content standards and Benchmarks for Social and Emotional Learning as the framework for instructional planning and assessment. This field-based course examines teaching theory and methods appropriate for the adolescent curriculum in Science, Mathematics, English Language Arts and Social Studies. Candidates will become familiar with the New York State Learning Standards and will develop the necessary planning skills and teaching strategies to be effective teachers in the 7-12 classroom. There is a 15-hour content-specific field experience component in this course.

title: Positive Behavioral Support for SWD
description: This course will provide candidates with an understanding of challenging behaviors and behavioral interventions and supports for students with disabilities (SWD), specifically students with low incidence disabilities. The course describes and analyzes the Positive Behavior Support (PBS) model utilized in most schools, and candidates will understand their roles and responsibilities as members of a Multi-Tiered System of Support (MTSS). Candidates in the course will also apply Functional Behavioral Assessments (FBA) and develop Behavioral Intervention Plans (BIP) to IEP design as part of the PBS model. Collaboration among stakeholders, team members, parents, paraprofessionals, administrators, and students, is emphasized in the course as a means to cultivate academic success for all.

title: Adolescent Literacy I
description: This course is the first in a two-part series aimed at equipping pre-service adolescent teachers with the knowledge and skills to help their future students develop the literacy strategies and skills essential for success in middle and high school content-area classes. The course introduces foundational theories and practical approaches to literacy instruction, including Science of Reading, tailored to meet the needs of students in contemporary classrooms. It examines how literacy practices develop and can be strengthened, as well as the role of critical literacy and new literacies in a globalized world. Throughout the course, preservice teachers will engage in activities focused on reading, writing, listening, and speaking to build their understanding of effective literacy instruction.

title: Education of Students with Low Incidence Disabilities
description: This course will provide candidates with a comprehensive review of current information about low incidence disabilities. This course draws on research findings and clinical experience from a number of related disciplines, including psychiatry, psychology, neurobiology and special education disability studies. This is also designed to help teachers develop, adapt and implement literacy and numeracy curriculum and assessments for students with severe and/or multiple disabilities. In addition, standards based and functional curriculum planning will be explored.

title: Societal Perspectives in Special Education â
description: This course focuses on the developmental, learning, behavioral, and social characteristics and needs of students at the childhood and adolescent level in grades 1-12 across the spectrum of disabilities. Etiological factors of disability, effective intervention strategies, and an understanding of societal perceptions related to determinations of disability are emphasized. The course includes discussion on the importance of school, family, and community collaboration in educating and including individuals with disabilities. Fifteen hours of field experience in local agencies serving individuals with disabilities rounds out the learning in this course.

title: Adolescent Literacy II
description: The follow-up course to EDU-357 emphasizes assessing literacy skills in adolescent learners and their application to content-area learning. Students will explore key theories and research on language structure and acquisition to support English language learners (ELLs) in developing language proficiency and achieving success in content areas. A significant focus of the course is on assessment, diagnosis, and remediation strategies. Students will apply their understanding of assessment principles by creating and conducting a case study in a clinical setting.

title: Educational Psychology â
description: This course focuses on the application of psychological findings and principles to the field of education. It explores the array of individual learning and behavioral differences found in the diverse student population of today's schools. Students will compare and contrast tenets of the primary theories of learning (behavioral, cognitive, developmental, constructivist and social).

title: Introductory Topics in Education
description: An introductory study of a specific topic in Education. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Early Childhood Methods
description: This course is an investigation into how to teach content knowledge, concepts and skills in the early childhood classroom (inclusive and general education), with an emphasis on using the New York State Early Learning Guidelines and Common Core State Standards as the framework for instructional planning, implementation, and assessment. This course focuses on instructional planning for and teaching of young children in an intentional, developmentally appropriate way utilizing a balance of child-initiated and adult-guided learning that integrates content areas, using hands-on learning, manipulatives, and inquiry-based learning activities and centers. Creating a supportive and purposefully planned environment for learning is also included. There is an 8-hour field experience in this course.

title: Assessment in Early Childhood Inclusive Settings
description: This course will look at procedures used to obtain information about an individual's development in academic and non-academic areas, and how to use that information to guide curriculum and planning. Attention will be given to the use of assessment results, from norm-referenced and standardized tests, for placement decisions and planning, and monitoring of IFSP & EIP goals and objectives for students with disabilities. There will be a ten-hour field experience in this course.

title: Teaching in Inclusive Schools
description: This course emphasizes the role of the special education teacher as decision-maker; one who manages the instructional/learning environment so as to achieve optimal outcomes for all students. Included in this course are the preparation of IEPs, Transition Plans, co-teaching strategies, communications with students, parents, peers and colleagues, test and instructional modifications, and the use of assistive technology. A distinct feature of this course is an examination of the need for collaboration between regular and special education professionals and the need to ensure positive learning outcomes for all students across P-12.

title: Collaborative Transition/Disabilities
description: This course will provide candidates with the opportunity to understand transition planning from a variety of roles/planner, transition manager/specialist, student advocate and service coordinator. Additionally, the candidate will develop an understanding of person-centered planning, strength-based inventories and applications as they relate to IEPs, transition plans and IPPs.

title: SWD All Grades Education Field Period
description: An off-campus classroom field experience with exposure to children having identified disabilities and exposure to alternative curriculum materials, accommodating strategies, behavior management techniques, special education instructional methodology and the professional role of the special educator. The supervisor must be a special educator. The chronological age of the children should reflect the extended grades needed for special education at all grades (Adolescent majors need to work in grades P-6, Childhood in grades 7-12, and Early Childhood in 7-12). This cannot be taken as a First-Year Field Period.

title: Teaching in Kindergarten
description: This course is an intense, deeply concentrated course of study across al the domains needed to become a knowledgeable kindergarten teacher.  The course builds upon the candidate's previous knowledge and experiences as they gain knowledge and skills necessary to create an appropriate learning environment in kindergarten.  Theoretical underpinnings, as well as planning and assessment techniques unique to teaching kindergarten are examined.  Related topics included in this course include: child development, inquiry- based thematic curriculum planning and implementation authentic formative assessment as well as informal screening assessment, appropriate and positive behavior guidance, and an examination of a foundational educational philosophy.

title: Special Education Field Period
description: An off-campus classroom experience with exposure to children having identified disabilities and exposure to alternative curriculum materials, accommodating strategies, behavior management techniques, special education instructional methodology and the professional role of the special educator. The supervisor must be a special educator. The chronological age of the children should reflect the age of desired certification. This cannot be taken as a freshman Field Period.

title: Education Related Field Period
description: An off-campus experience in an education-related setting to explore roles and responsibilities of education personnel other than the regular or special educator, or to work children in a non-classroom setting. Appropriate for Education Studies capstone Field Period. Does not fulfill either of the two required education Field Periods.

title: Professional Management Issues
description: This course provides an opportunity for pre-service teachers to integrate knowledge and skills learned previously and develop a personal teaching style. Some topics include classroom management techniques, application of teaching methodologies, provisions for individualization of instruction for special needs students, and working with parents & families. All aspects of this course help prepare the students for student teaching and their eventual induction into the teaching profession.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Instructional Design â
description: Students examine rationally consistent basis for the design, development, and evaluation of instruction based on cognitive psychology and information-processing theory. This course will prepare prospective instructors to design and develop a course, unit, and module of instruction, and evaluate learner outcomes. Using the nine stages of instructional design procedure and integrating current research and practice in performance systems technology, this course provides applicable skills in any discipline where teaching or training is required. Likewise, this course emphasizes the social and cultural context of learning, learner-centered principles, and the affordances of new technologies, digital learning, and learning environments.

title: Student Teaching Seminar
description: This seminar is an accompaniment to student teaching, providing students with the opportunity to discuss student teaching experiences, challenges and celebrations, and gain valuable skills in becoming a reflective practitioner.  In addition, teacher certification procedures, development of a professional portfolio and job interview techniques are addressed to prepare students for the job search process.

title: Student Teaching in Childhood and Special Education
description: Fourteen-week sequence of supervised  student teaching in elementary and special education.

title: Student Teaching in Adolescent and Special Education
description: Fourteen-week sequence of supervised student teaching in secondary and special education.

title: Student Teaching in Adolescent Education
description: Fourteen-week sequence of supervised student teaching in area 7-9 and 10-12 classrooms.

title: Student Teaching in Early Childhood and Special Education
description: Fourteen-week sequence of supervised student teaching in preschool, kindergarten, or grades 1-2, and special education.

title: Advanced Topics in Education
description: An advanced study of a specific topic in Education. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Theoretical and Social Found of Literacy
description: The focus of this course is to lay the groundwork for making evidence-based instructional literacy decisions, and to begin the process of appreciating the need to communicate complex ideas to multiple stakeholders in literacy education. To accomplish these goals, students will: understand current theoretical perspectives in literacy education and literacy acquisition (such as the Piagetian and Vygotskian theories); understand the impact of research and diversity on literacy education; explore the impact literacy and lack of literacy have on individuals; and explore the social-cultural implications surrounding the competing theories of literacy education. To accomplish these goals, the course examines: the evolution of theories of learning that apply to literacy; research that has contributed to evolving models and definitions of reading; and the contributions of theories of literacy (reading and writing) as language-based processes. The course examines this information in the context of social, cultural, and political milieu of education in the United States. An important factor in understanding and using theories is the ability to recognize one's own history, biases and beliefs, and how these impact one's own interpretation and implementation of knowledge. This course begins with students engaging in a self-reflective review of their own literacy learning and beliefs, and how that history and those beliefs affect their literacy education beliefs.

title: Practicum All Grades
description: This supervised field experience in PreK-4 and 5-12 grades combines practice, demonstration, self-evaluation, and validation of competencies gained in the literacy program. Students will be teaching literacy under the supervision of a college faculty member.

title: Introduction to Educational Research
description: An introduction to the rationale and procedures of educational research, placing primary emphasis on literacy research. The intent of the course is to help the student become an intelligent consumer of research, and to develop an appreciation for the importance and utility of research. Students will develop critical skills needed as consumers and producers of research. Topics include: the nature and purposes of research, critiquing and evaluation of research, reviewing research literature, valid measurement issues, the use of statistics in research, and types of research. Content from this course will lay the foundation for all courses throughout the program.

title: Professional Leadership in Early Childhood
description: This course covers knowledge of how to  administer and direct a child care program. Students will study legal requirements, guidelines established by professional organizations, ways to obtain additional professional training, how to network with other professional organizations, referral agencies and funding sources, how to maintain quality early childhood care and education programs and how to follow a professional code of ethics.  Students conduct in-depth research of a challenge in the early childhood field.  Developing strong, reflective ECE leaders and advocates for young children and their families pervades this course.  There is a ten-hour field experience component associated with this course.

title: Applied Educational Studies Capstone
description: This course serves as a capstone.  Taught seminar style, this course synthesizes the learning experiences of the Applied Educational Studies major by focusing on real world application of the theories, principles, and practices of instructional design with which students have been working.  In the course, students serve as consultants and move through the phases of developing a learning module for their clients: meeting and discussing strategies with clients, developing and implementing a needs assessment using survey instrumentation (Babbie), creating outcomes and designing learning experiences, and assessing the quality of the learning and any changes in behaviors on the part of the participants.  The capstone would include a presentation to the class (and to the client) as well as a presentation to the Education Division and to the Keuka College community.  An internship in a professional setting where a client is defined and solicited for purposes of the capstone project, is included in this course.

title: Regular Education Classroom Field Period
description: An off-campus classroom experience with exposure to curriculum materials, classroom management, instructional planning, methodology and the professional role of the regular educator. The chronological age of the children should reflect the area of desired certification. This cannot be taken as a freshman Field Period.

title: Assessment-Based Instruction PreK-2
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of teaching methods and materials relevant to literacy instruction for the emergent and developing readers in today's diverse classrooms. This course will examine both the nature and function of formal and informal assessment processes in literacy instruction and best instructional practice that emanates from this assessment. In order to both design literacy instructional programs and to assess student learning, it is vital to be able to place students along a developmental continuum and identify students' proficiencies and areas needing strengthening. Awareness of the influence of culture, class, gender, and context on literacy acquisition informs the teacher's knowledge base when considering this developmental continuum. Language development and its relationship to literacy acquisition will also be examined. Teachers will learn a wide range of instructional practices, strategies, and methods to support reading and writing instruction. Teachers will also demonstrate competency in using a wide range of formal and informal assessment tools and practices that provide data used to craft appropriate teaching strategies for addressing student strengths and needs, and design remedial interventions. Teachers will develop the skills necessary to ensure that students experience developmentally appropriate growth in literacy skills. In addition, teachers develop skills to identify students who are not making developmentally appropriate growth, and also learn the appropriate strategies to recover those skills. Course includes 10 hours of clinical teaching experience.

title: Literacy in the Digital Age
description: This course is designed to provide candidates with knowledge of materials and technology as instructional tools for literacy learning in all grades. Candidates will learn about uses of instructional and assistive technology, and learn to incorporate technology into teaching and learning to acquire information, communicate and enhance learning for all students. This course will include assessing student interest, evaluating the developmental levels, qualities, and efficacy of materials and technology, and consideration of instructional frameworks for technology-enhanced literacy instruction for all grades.

title: Assessment-Based Instruction 2-6
description: This course identifies goals for data driven literacy instruction for students in elementary grades two through six. The goals include: (1) understanding literacy development and learning, with special application to students at these grade levels; (2) determining individual needs for instruction in literacy skills and strategies with a variety of assessment tools, both formal and informal; and (3) using assessment data and knowledge of development to select and implement instructional strategies and curriculum materials that will facilitate progress in literacy for all students. The intent of these goals is to provide teachers with the skills necessary to facilitate appropriate, timely literacy development in students, as well as to identify and resolve issues that impede progress in literacy. Participants in this course learn to implement a literacy program based on best practices while addressing the needs of older, struggling readers and writers. The course continues awareness of the influence of culture, class, race, ethnicity, gender, and historical and geographical contexts on literacy acquisition. Course includes 10 hours of clinical teaching experience.

title: Professional Leadership in Literacy Education
description: An important component of leadership in literacy education is the ability to evaluate, plan, and conduct research. This course will begin to provide students with skills to be effective leaders for literacy education. These skills include: (1) identification of current concerns or issues in literacy; (2) evaluation of research surrounding the issue or concern; (3) planning the implementation of an action to address the issue or concern; and (4) communicating the research-based plan to others. As a culminating activity in this course, each student will prepare a draft capstone proposal (as defined by the Graduate Handbook), and present that proposal to peers and other stakeholders of the program. A second component of literacy leadership is the ability to advocate for effective literacy education. Therefore, an additional goal of this course is to provide students with the opportunity to develop their leadership and advocacy skills.

title: Assessment-Based Literacy Instruction III
description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of teaching methods and materials relevant to literacy instruction for the readers in today's diverse 5-12 classrooms. This course will examine both the nature and function of formal and informal assessment processes in literacy instruction and best instructional practice that emanates from this assessment. In order to both design literacy instructional programs and to assess student learning, it is vital to be able to identify students' proficiencies and areas needing strengthening. Awareness of the influence of culture, class, gender, and context on literacy acquisition informs the teacher's knowledge base when addressing these needs. Teachers will learn a wide range of instructional practices, strategies, and methods to support reading and writing instruction. Teachers will also demonstrate competency in using a wide range of both formal and informal assessment tools and practices that provide data used to craft appropriate teaching strategies for addressing student strengths and needs in the 5-12 classroom. In addition, teachers develop skills to identify students who are not making appropriate growth, and also learn the appropriate strategies to recover those skills.

title: Literacy for Diverse Learners
description: Course will build on candidates' existing knowledge of working with diverse learners, and expand on it with topics related to exploring strategies for addressing a number of sociocultural frameworks looking at issues such as diversity, race, culture, language, and social hierarchies, and their connections to literacy. Candidates will engage in identifying appropriate instructional approaches and literacy services for students who are diverse in race, ethnicity, culture, language, social class, religion, disability, gender, sexual orientation, and ability. Current research contributing to our understanding of working with diverse learners and literacy will be examined.

title: Practica III
description: This supervised 50-hour practicum in grades 5-12 settings provides practice, demonstration, self-reflection and validation of competencies gained in the literacy program. This course involves a reflective tutoring experience with students in grades 5-12 in both individual and group settings. As this course is taken concurrently with or following EDU 541, students will be applying and practicing the goals and objectives stated in EDU 541. Students will be in the practicum for a minimum of 50 hours. Practicing educators must do the practicum outside of their regular classroom.

title: Practica 3-6
description: This supervised field experience in a 3-6 setting provides practice, demonstration, self-evaluation and validation of competencies gained in the literacy program. Students under the supervision of a college faculty member will be expected to plan for and practice the course objectives listed in EDU 534 Instruction and Assessment II. Students will be in the practicum for a minimum of 30 hours.

title: Content Area Literacy 5-12
description: This course is designed to highlight the role of literacy assessment, acquisition, and application within the content areas in grades 5-12. It will provide an in-depth exploration of formal and informal assessment methods, teaching methods and materials relevant to literacy instruction for the content areas and students in today's diverse classrooms. Literacy and writing strategies will be presented in conjunction with New York State Learning Standards. An additional focus of the course will be to help literacy and subject area teachers address the needs of diverse learners of all abilities through culturally relevant pedagogy and curriculum, the use of multicultural materials, and alternative and adaptive methods, and the use of technology. Course includes 10 hours of clinical teaching experience.

title: Professional Seminar 3
description: This is a course designed to facilitate the professional completion of graduate student's capstone projects. In this course, students will complete the final editing, by peer and external reader, of the project, preparation for publication in the Division's capstone compilation. This includes the preparation of an abstract, professional editing, the creation of a professional presentation as well as the attendant materials. This is a pass/fail course.

title: Student Teaching in Adolescent Generalist Special Education
description: Fourteen-week sequence of supervised student teaching in adolescent special education placements, one placement at each developmental grade level: grades 7-9 and 10-12.

title: Research Seminar I
description: This course is designed to facilitate the research of graduate students in the MS in Education Literacy B-6 and 5-12 as they write their capstone papers. In this course the students will prepare Parts 1 and 2 of their capstone projects as well as complete the IRB proposal. This is a pass/fail course.

title: Capstone Seminar
description: This is a seminar-style course that provides instruction and support to group and individual students as they integrate the research information gained regarding literacy instruction, and refine their capstone project. Students will share ideas, concerns, and drafts of their final project for peer review. This research seminar is the terminal experience in the master's degree program. As such, it represents a synthesis and culmination of the research in literacy instruction, the application of research-based best practices, and reflection on the efficacy of their instructional practices and decision making.

title: Writing as Conversation â *
description: This course provides students with focused study of process-based writing and emphasizes critical reading and the ability to respond clearly and effectively to a variety of rhetorical situations. Students learn to read as writers and write for an assortment of purposes and audiences. Through holistic instruction and weekly workshops, students actively engage in and produce effective writing.

title: Writing as Inquiry â *
description: This course focuses on writing as a means of discovery and inquiry. Students read and examine texts in order to evaluate the choices writers make and to develop their own perspectives and rhetorical authority. By exploring how writing functions in a range of academic, professional, and public contexts, students gain practice composing in a variety of modalities. Students give and receive feedback, revise their work, reflect on their growth as writers, and improve basic digital and information literacy skills.

title: HONORS: Writing as Argument â *
description: This course focuses on writing as research-based argument. As a means of arriving at and writing from committed stances, student writers learn to seek out, interrogate, and effectively use a variety of popular and academic sources. Students write in academic and professional modes, putting their own ideas in conversation with the ideas of others, and shaping arguments for multiple purposes and audiences. Particular emphasis is placed on advanced digital and information literacy skills. The Honors section of this course includes additional experiential learning, field-based research, and the production of a public-facing composition.

title: Storytelling â *
description: We will examine how stories represent and respond to the human experience and develop skills of interpretation, analysis, and critical evaluation.

title: Best Practices in Teaching Writing
description: In this course students learn about the development of writing, theories about writing, and effective instructional practices to use with children, birth through grade six. Throughout the course, the focus in on communicative intent, with an awareness of the conventions (spelling, grammar, punctuation, handwriting, etc.) that children must learn and use in order to carry out that intent. Emergent writing skills and the spring ties of writing to oral language development are examined. Participants learn how to implement a process approach to writing, scaffolding the process and expectations of the learner to changing developmental levels of students as they mature. Participants also learn to construct learning environments that support and facilitate the development of writing strategies and skills, using peer as well as teacher assistance. Process in this course is approaches as a recursive, individual approach to writing, rather than a linear, formulaic sequence of activities. Varied purposes for writing are explored along with the craft skills that writers use to enhance their writing. Application of writing across the subject areas is also stressed.

title: Great Books â *
description: This course examines great works of literature that shaped or have been shaped by the Western literary tradition. Representative texts selected from the Judeo-Christian scriptures, the classical tradition of Greek and Roman literature, and/or from the early modern period to the present. This course evaluates works through understanding cultural contexts, influences, and genres.

title: Poems, Plays and Prose â *
description: An introduction to the three most recognizable genres of literature: poetry, drama, and prose. Course focuses on reading texts from a variety of historical periods, cultural traditions, and critical perspectives. Approach may be topical or thematic and may vary from semester to semester.

title: The Language of Film â *
description: This course treats the script to screen process as a problem to be solved. Students study the language of film and filmmaking in order to understand how stories, words, and ideas are translated into images and actions on the big screen. By focusing specifically on films developed from books and plays, students learn to evaluate, interpret, and analyze the purpose, context, and audience of textual and visual media.

title: American Academic Culture â
description: This intermediate level academic English course is intended for nonnative English speaking international students entering Keuka College as Junior or Senior level transfer students or who have already completed the basic English requirements. The course provides nonnative English speaking international students with the skills and strategies needed to meet the academic demands of their courses in a US institution of higher education. The focus is primarily on academic reading, listening, and speaking, according to students' needs. A grade of C- or higher is required to pass this course.

title: The English Language â
description: This course provides a compelling look into the nature and uses of the English language. It addresses such questions as: Where does English come from? How does the language work? How and why has English changed over time? How have authors made use of, and extended the capabilities of, the English language?

title: Integrated Project Seminar
description: This seminar is a culminating experience of the graduate program. Candidates will synthesize their understanding of a literacy specialist's role by designing and developing an integrated project that is of interest to them. The project will represent candidates' interpretation of current literacy research, supportive instructional design for literacy practices, and their application. Candidates will participate in a presentation of their project to an audience of college faculty, peers, and invited guests.

title: HONORS: Literature of New York State â *
description: This course focuses on works of imaginative literature from the state of New York and challenges students to consider the implications and consequences of historical and cultural constructions of place. Through reading, discussing, and writing about literature that specifically considers human interactions with their physical environments, students explore the means by which the natural world is understood and represented, and they interrogate the ways physical spaces are reorganized into cultural places. The Honors section includes additional experiential learning, co-curricular elements, and applied community activities, including trips to visit locations depicted in the literature.

title: Principles of Creative Writing â *
description: This course introduces student writers to the fundamentals of writing fiction and poetry. Through instructor facilitated discussion and workshops, student writers analyze models, experiment with technique and form, review and discuss peer compositions, and cultivate their own writing voices.

title: Research Seminar 2
description: This course is designed to facilitate graduate students in the completion of their capstone projects. In this course, the students will completed parts 3, 4, and 5 of their capstone projects. This is a pass-fail course.

title: Children's Literature â
description: A survey of quality literature past and present for children and young adults.

title: Writing and Communication Arts Field Period
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to apply his/her academic knowledge to a real-career setting.

title: Reading the World â
description: This course is about reading. It is not just about reading to comprehend. It is about interpretation, analysis, and the use of material, visual, and digital texts. How do art, architecture, clothing, media, and music influence what we do, believe, and think? How do history and culture shape what we see and read? How do we use culture and ideology to persuade others to change their minds or take action? This course introduces and puts into practice the fundamentals of rhetorical theory and analysis. Emphasis is placed on applying critical and rhetorical perspectives to contemporary contexts and artifacts—art, music, film and television, digital and social media.

title: Creative Nonfiction â
description: Advanced study and practice of forms of creative nonfiction, such as the essay, literary journalism, travel writing, and memoir.

title: Studies in British Literature â
description: An examination of British works of prose, poetry, drama, and fiction in relation to their historical, linguistic, and cultural contexts.

title: Editing and Publishing â
description: This course focuses on the fundamentals of print and digital editing and publishing. Students develop line editing and peer review skills, explore the various roles of editors and publishers, and examine the ways writers negotiate the complex world of publishing. Familiarizes students with a range of writing-related careers.

title: Writing and Communication Arts Field Period
description: Off-campus experiential education opportunity that allows the student to apply his/her academic knowledge to a real-career setting.

title: Advanced Studies in Literature â
description: Advanced study of a topic, theme, or genre  through intensive study of representative works of literature.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Environmental Science â *
description: An exploration of interrelationships of environmental factors and how they influence and are influenced by national and global environmental problems.

title: Literature and Society in Latin America â
description: An introduction to the study of literature through the analysis of representative works from the contemporary (20th and 21st centuries) narrative tradition of Latin America. Analysis will focus not only on literary perspectives but also engage with historical, social, cultural, and geographic frameworks.

title: Lab: Environmental Science
description: Lab for ENV 105.

title: Lab: Introductory Topics in Environmental Science
description: Lab for ENV-295

title: Natural Resource Conservation â
description: This course provides a broad overview of natural resource conservation with particular emphasis on soil, water, and biodiversity. Principles of natural resource conservation, management techniques, current issues, and the history of conservation will be investigated.

title: Introductory Topics in Environmental Science â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Environmental Science. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Environmental Field Techniques â
description: This field course offers an introduction to the identification of organisms relevant to environmental science, as well as survey techniques, sampling, and analysis. Emphasis will be on local ecosystems and sampling and analytical techniques currently employed by regional environmental science-related agencies.

title: Studies in American Literature â
description: An examination of a variety of perspectives that define American literature in relation to their historical, linguistic, and cultural contexts.

title: Ecology â
description: A qualitative and quantitative study of the interrelationships of organisms and the environment; lecture and laboratory.

title: Introductory Topics in English â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within English. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Geographic Information Systems â
description: This course offers an introduction to concepts of Geographic Information Systems, geospatial analysis, and visualization. GIS skills will be developed with hands-on use of leading software.

title: Lab: Geographic Information Systems
description: Lab for ENV-310

title: Lab: Environmental Field Techniques
description: Lab for ENG-320 .

title: Lab: Ecology
description: Lab for ENV 337.

title: Lab: Advanced Environmental Science
description: Lab for ENV 351.

title: Seminar in Scientific Communication â
description: Instruction in critical reading and analysis of recent primary and secondary source journal articles in the scientific literature. Students will develop and present literature critiques of research articles relevant to their discipline in biology, biochemistry, or environmental science.

title: Advanced Topics in English â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within English. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in English. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Carbon, Climate and Energy â
description: This course provides a deeper look into the nature of the global climate system. Students will examine the factors influencing climate, including interactions among the atmosphere, oceans, solid earth, and biosphere. Aspects of the carbon cycle and transfer of energy between systems will be explored, with a focus on impacts due to human activities.

title: Undergraduate Research
description: Opportunity for students to conduct  investigative study in the laboratory and/or field or to perform some type of creative work not covered by other courses. The student will work with a faculty member to develop a research proposal, and carry out the proposed work. Completed Application for Research and Research Proposal forms are required. The course will culminate with the preparation of a research report. May be repeated.

title: Senior Seminar in Natural Science â
description: This is considered the capstone course for students completing majors within the natural sciences. The course focuses on instruction in critical thinking, analysis and information literacy in the science field and current topics in modern science. Each student will write a substantial research paper on a scientific area of interest using knowledge from previous coursework. Each student will complete a public presentation of his/her topic.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Senior Research â
description: Opportunity for senior students to conduct original investigative study in the laboratory and/or field, or to perform creative work of the type not covered by other courses. The student will work with a faculty member to develop a research proposal, and carry out the proposed work. Completed Application for Research and Research Proposal forms are required. The work may be a continuation of a project started in BIO/CHM/ENV 398 or another course.  The project will culminate in the preparation of a formal research report. May be repeated.

title: Advanced Topics in Environmental Science â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Environmental Science. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Introduction to Esports Management
description: This is an introductory course for sports and Esports management. Students will be taught the basic principles of administrative and management concepts associated with sports and Esports. Descriptions of career opportunities are provided to help the student design a course of study that best meets his/her career goals.

title: Lab: Advanced Topics in Environmental Science
description: Lab for ENV-495

title: Advanced Environmental Science â
description: A study of the biology, chemistry, and geology of the Earth. An examination of the composition and reactions of the oceans, atmosphere, crustal minerals, and living organisms. Modeling of elemental/molecular reservoirs and transfers will be an important component of the laboratories.

title: Esports Management I
description: An overview of the sports and Esports management industries focusing on identification and applying theoretical knowledge to real-world actions. The course will dive into professional sports, as well as collegiate and introduce how the emergence of Esports is impacting the sports industry. Content will include sales, marketing and promotions, and public relations and communications as it pertains to sports.

title: Esports Management Senior Capstone
description: Selected independent study of design project with approval of departmental personnel. Students are able to apply classroom theory to practical experience.

title: Introductory Topics in Esports Management
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Esports Management. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Lab: Limnology
description: Lab for ENV 360.

title: Esports Management II
description: An in-depth focus and practical application of sports and Esports management.  to include strategic planning, finance, event management, and personnel management. Course content will include strategic planning, event management, personnel management, finance, marketing, and emerging technologies in sports/Esports. Upon completion of this course, you will understand the competencies necessary for managing and operating sports and Esports events.

title: Financial Management
description: An examination of the topics of traditional financial management, with an emphasis on working capital management, short-term financing, leverage, capital structure, and discounted cash flow techniques.

title: Foundations for Adult and Experiential Learning â
description: Foundations for Adult and Experiential Learning will introduce students to the scholarly demands of Keuka College and provide an understanding of the liberal arts for adult students in higher education.  Students will critically evaluate their educational journey and professional goals through the exploration of adult learning theory, philosophies of education, and learning styles.  This course will utilize critical reading, active discussion, and reflective writing to help adult students become a contributing member of an academic community of learners.

title: International Financial Management
description: This course involves the study of international financial markets including exchange rate determination, exchange rate risk management and currency derivatives. The topics it will cover include: international taxation, trade agreements, and foreign investments.

title: Financial Institutions
description: A study of the financial institutions of our society, such as banks, stock and bond markets, and the Federal Reserve. The function of financial intermediaries and regulatory agencies is covered, as well as portfolio and capital market theory.

title: Environmental Science Field Period
description: A broad range of opportunities  exist for environmental science majors to experience real-world applications in their field of study. This Field Period is intended to enhance career opportunities and examine, in depth, specific areas of interest. Areas of interest can vary from political issues to highly specialized laboratory and field techniques. This Field Period must be evaluated by a faculty member in the Natural Sciences.

title: Introductory Topics in Finance
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Finance. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Investments
description: This course provides a solid foundation in investment and securities analysis. Topics covered in this course include elements of investments, portfolio theory, debt securities, equities, security analysis, derivative markets, investment processes, behavioral finance, and technical analysis. Students learn how to generate their own investment strategies using Exchange Traded Funds (“ETFs”).

title: Limnology â
description: An interdisciplinary study of inland waters: lakes, streams, rivers, estuaries and wetlands. Water topics to be studied include: unique characteristics, distribution, origins, and the biological, physical and chemical composition. A primary focus will be on the ecological and economical importance of water and human impacts. Laboratory and field work are required.

title: Money and Banking
description: Money and banking emphasizes the financial system, financial institutions, central banks, monetary policy and financial stability.  The course studies the role that money and interest rates play in the operation of the U.S. economy.  The aim is to give students an overview of the U.S. financial system, and an understanding of the theory and practice of monetary policy. The course looks at the changing local, national and the global financial environment and the influence of monetary policy on interest rates, prices and the overall level of economic activity.

title: Honors Program Field Period
description: The Honors Program Field Period is an experiential learning opportunity in a vocational, cultural, or community service setting and has an objective aligned with the Honors Program.

title: Advanced Topics in Esports Management
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Esports Management. Intended for juniors and seniors or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Group Field Period
description: Special Topics: An off-campus group activity that provides students with the opportunity to experience diverse socio-cultural and/or natural environments.  Students work with the faculty sponsor(s) of the group Field Period to establish individual learning objectives.

title: Prior Experiential Learning Field Period
description: Informal learning is assessed by documenting the learning that took place in the past, and by relating the knowledge and competencies gained to theoretical frameworks normally included in college coursework.

title: Sociology of Aging â
description: The purpose of this course is to study the area of gerontology that is concerned with impact of social and socio-cultural conditions on the process of aging and with the social consequences of this process. It will explore how the older population and the varieties of aging experiences both affect and are affected by the social structure. Questions about what societal resources will be required to support the growing older population.

title: Career Experiential Learning - Field Period
description: This course gives students practical hands-on experience in the form of Field Periods that integrates classroom learning and real world experience.  Students work with employers through off-campus experiences to gain real world experience in career management and reflect on it in class.  Keuka College graduation requirements include passing the Field Period assessment done by Keuka College.

title: Introduction to Social Gerontology â
description: This comprehensive course provides an overview of the field of gerontology. We are living in an aging society, one in which the older population is growing both in absolute numbers and proportion to all other age groups. This aging is accompanied by a demand for new products and services appropriate to the needs of older people. This multidisciplinary course will assist the student in understanding physical, mental and social changes in older people as they age. It will include the investigation of the changes in society resulting from our diverse aging population. This course also introduces students to such salient topics as the common myths and stereotypes about aging and older adults. Opportunity for experiential learning is made possible through a service-learning component. Students serve the elderly by working with the residents at a local long-term facility.

title: Perspectives on Aging â
description: An intensive study of aging from an interdisciplinary perspective. The purpose of this course is to contribute knowledge essential for meeting the challenges of an aging society. An examination of the profound effect on individuals, families, communities and society as a whole. Family relationships, the composition of the work force, housing needs, healthcare services, public policies, and the marketing of products and services are explored. Opportunity for experiential learning is made possible through a service-learning component. Students serve in a variety of community agencies that are part of the Aging Network.

title: Contemporary Issues in Gerontology â
description: Many institutional policies and programs are in place to deal with individual aging but our society needs to begin to address the issues generated by population aging. During the 20th century nearly three decades have been added to human life expectancy. Today nearly a third of adult life may be spent in retirement. This course will allow the student to study the economics and the policies of aging. The challenge is to reexamine the policies and practices, such as retirement, pensions and other familiar government programs such as social security and Medicare.

title: Health Care for the Older Adult â
description: Recent improvements in health care and disease prevention mean that most people will remain in relatively good health. This course will provide the nurse and other health care workers essential knowledge and tools to assist the older adults and their families remain physically, emotionally and spiritually well. Students will explore the influence of gender, race, ethnicity and socioeconomic status on health. Diseases common to the older adult will also be studied and how to best assist the older adult to live with a chronic illness.

title: Field Work in Gerontology â
description: This practicum offers the certificate program students a critical opportunity to design a learning experience suitable to their individual learning goals. The possible areas of study may include but are not limited to healthcare, recreation, social service, administration and long term care. Students are directed to explore topics of professional interest to them. The practicum experience requires a minimum of 140 clock hours. The gerontology program coordinator will work with students to select appropriate field sites and develop a customized learning contract, which includes educational outcomes and assessment strategies.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Europe and the World â *
description: This course surveys the political, military, and socio-economic developments of Europe from the French Revolution to the end of the Cold War.

title: World History â
description: An examination of the major developments that have shaped the human experience from the earliest civilizations to the present day.  Special attention will be paid to the overall patterns of global history, characteristics of the world's major pre-modern civilizations, and the relationships and exchanges between and among the world's societies.

title: Advanced Topics in Gerontology
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Gerontology. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Advanced Topics in Finance
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Finance. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: United States History from 1877-Present â *
description: A survey of American history from the end of Reconstruction to the early twenty-first century.

title: Introductory Topics in History â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within History. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Reading the Past: Method and Analysis â *
description: An introduction to the ways historians think about the world and interpret the past.  The course will highlight various methodologies that historians employ in their work, and train students in  research and writing using primary and secondary sources.  Each semester, special emphasis will be placed on the analysis of historical and literary texts that illustrate a specific theme.

title: United States History to 1877 â *
description: A survey of American history from first contact between indigenous peoples and Europeans through the Civil War and Reconstruction.

title: America in the Founding Era, 1750-1815 â
description: This course explores the creation of the United States and the complex political, social, economic, and cultural dynamics that shaped American society in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries.  Special emphasis will be placed on the American Revolution and the Constitutional Convention.

title: Psychology of Aging â
description: This course examines psychological development of old age. Perceptual, cognitive, learning, and motivational processes are studied in relation to the physical changes that individuals experience and the social worlds they inhabit in adulthood. Personality, morality, sex-role, intelligence, intimacy and careers are some of the topics considered within a developmental perspective.

title: Strategic Systems Programs of the United States Navy Internship
description: This internship opportunity is sponsored and operated by the Strategic Systems Programs of the United States Navy, which directs the development, production, logistics support, and sustaining engineering effort of the Navy Strategic Weapons Systems. The internship will provide undergraduate students with an introduction to federal employment via on-the-job training. The intern will primarily assist naval officers in the Political Affairs Office of Strategic Systems Programs who prepare briefings for the U.S. Department of Defense and testify in committee hearings conducted by the U.S. House of Representatives and U.S. Senate.  Those interns who move on to the Developmental Employee Program will gain real world experience while working towards a full-time position after they graduate from college.

title: History of New York State â
description: This course examines the social, political, economic, and cultural history of New York State from the Iroquois, through the Dutch and English colonial periods, to the 21st century.

title: The U.S. Civil War and Reconstruction â
description: This course will examine the causes, fighting, and outcomes of the U.S. Civil War and Reconstruction. Combining lectures, readings, primary source analysis ,films, and discussion, the course will address such questions as why the war came, why the United States won (or the Confederacy lost), and how the war affected various elements of American society. Although this is not a course on Civil War battles and generals, a significant portion of the class will be devoted to military affairs.  There will be a special effort to tie events on the battlefield to life behind the lines in both the North and the Confederacy.

title: Introductory Topics in Gerontology
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Gerontology. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Issues in Death and Dying â
description: Dying is the last stage of living. The dying process is as individual as each person is unique. This course will help the student develop a positive attitude toward death and dying and provide a knowledge base for working with individuals and their families. Medical technology has the capacity to prolong life. This and the changing legal and moral attitudes have contributed to many ethical issues and dilemmas. These will be investigated and possible solutions explored. An experiential learning component involving hospice and palliative care is a requirement of this course.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Introductory Topics in Human Resource Management
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Human Resource Management. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Human Resources Training and Development
description: This course is concerned with the theory and research of the systematic development and evaluation of training programs in a variety of organizational settings. The course provides a theoretical as well as practical orientation to the field.

title: Advanced Topics in History â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within History. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Human Resources Management
description: Aspects of the human resources function operable in business organizations; analysis of employment personnel testing, job classification, wage and salary administration, and performance evaluation; overview of labor relations in American business.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Senior Seminar in Human Resource Management
description: The case method technique is used to familiarize future managers with issues that involve designing and administering a system for hiring, advancing, and rewarding workers. The issues covered reflect current concerns of human resource management professionals (e.g., affirmative action, comparable worth, the aging work force, termination, and early retirement). The class functions as a seminar with students leading discussion of topics and cases.

title: International Studies Field Period
description: Field Period is an experiential learning opportunity in a vocational, cultural, or community service setting.

title: United States in World Affairs â
description: An examination of the foreign policy of the United States during the past 100 years, with special emphasis placed on America’s role in the world after the Second World War.

title: International Studies Field Period
description: Field Period is an experiential learning opportunity in a vocational, cultural, or community service setting.

title: Introductory Topics in International Studies
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within International Studies. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Practicing Academic Culture @Keuka â *
description: As the foundation of the general education Compass Curriculum, this course introduces first-time students to Keuka College's signature emphasis on experiential and integrated learning. Students explore how their diverse educational experiences contribute to their development as citizens and leaders. Students and faculty build a dynamic community by engaging in shared academic topics with individual sections guided by the instructor's area of expertise.

title: Advanced Topics in International Studies â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within International Studies. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Wellness: Life Design â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This course is an interactive journey into life design and explores essential topics for thriving in college and beyond. Possible subjects include financial skills, navigating civic responsibilities, legal know-how, personal growth, and building a resilient, growth-focused mindset. By the end of this course, students will have a personalized toolkit for success across all areas of their life, grounded in wellness and designed by themselves.

title: Wellness: Learning Leadership Through the Outdoors â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic.
