Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This Wellness course is designed to explore, analyze and facilitate the concept that holistic wellness is crucial to effective leadership, and the idea that the natural environment can directly affect one’s wellness. The course examines models, theories, and foundational background utilized in leadership development and application, as well as in adventure, experiential, and outdoor education including a historical overview of the stewardship of public and private lands.

title: Experiential Learning and Academic Culture @Keuka * â
description: As the foundation of the general education Compass Curriculum, this course introduces transfer students to Keuka College's signature emphasis on experiential and integrated learning. The course serves to introduce students to our dynamic academic community, connecting students to college culture and preparing them for their initial Field Period® experience.

title: Wellness: Journaling to be Well â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This course is designed to explore and apply the practice of journaling to develop better overall wellness and well-being. Although the focus of the course is on reflection through regular journal writing, opportunities will be provided for understanding and engaging with different kinds of journaling activities such as food journals, exercise journals, and dream journals and their benefits.  In addition to writing and reflecting, students will integrate their understanding of journaling with the journal work of thinkers and writers such as Leonardo DaVinci, Henry David Thoreau, Virginia Woolf, and Albert Camus.

title: Wellness: Physical Activity and Life Balance â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This course will explore a variety of physical activities and sports that can be used in an integrated approach to various dimensions of wellness. A range of physical activities and their specific effects on wellness will be introduced through research, lecture and small group discussion. Results of individual assessment tools will provide students with a basis for the development of an individual wellness plan. Students will be asked to compare and contrast an array of activities within an exploration of scientific, psychological, and sociological research to support their conclusions.

title: Strategic Compensation Management
description: This course studies and analyzes the basic concepts of compensation administration in organizations. Topics such as the foundation for wage and salary programs, methods of job evaluation, building wage and salary structures, and the legal constraints placed upon compensation programs are studied.

title: Industrial Labor Relations
description: A study of current problems faced in the area of labor relations. The course examines management and union philosophies, strategies, and tactics used in the collective bargaining process through the use of interviewing techniques and case studies.

title: Wellness: Origami â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This wellness course explores the art of paper folding. The activities and pastimes that we pursue outside of school and work support our intellectual, emotional, and physical wellness. Among enthusiasts, origami is frequently referred to as "the peace of paper." As this phrase implies, origami can be relaxing. It can also provide intellectual and creative challenges. Students will explore both traditional and modern origami models, learn about origami artists, explore the process of creating and diagraming original work, learn about the historical development of origami, and teach origami to others.

title: Financial Wellness * â
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This course helps students understand and manage their personal finances during college and after graduation. Students will learn basic budgeting, tax, and credit management concepts while acquiring the skills to manage their resources and untangle the seemingly complex world of personal finances. Studies have shown taking care of one’s financial wellness leads to enhanced emotional and mental well-being, self-confidence, life satisfaction, motivation, and positive stress management. This course focuses students on making sound financial decisions that can positively impact their well-being.

title: Wellness: Sexuality and Health â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. The purpose guides students to explore the relationship between sexuality, wellness, and health, including sexual behaviors and reproductive health. Students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of healthy sexual behaviors and sexual relationships. Students will be guided through self-assessment and reflection to identify areas in their life where the practice of sexual wellness will be most beneficial, including gender-related issues, consent and communication, the emotional and spiritual aspects of sexual relationships, prevention and reporting of sexual assault, and awareness of the wide range of human sexual behavior.

title: Advanced Topics in Human Resource Management
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Human Resource Management. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Wellness: Art and Community * â
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit This course will address a range of topics related to art and community across various dimensions of wellness. A cross-cultural framework will be employed. Specific topics may include color activities, sketching, painting, photography, poetry, typography, as well as reflection practices to promote personal development, collaboration, and a growth mindset.

title: Wellness: Who Am I? â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This course will help students explore “who they are” as a result of, among other factors, their family history, race, culture, religion, and political leanings. Through personal assessment and research, classroom discussion, and reflective journaling, students will identify how their background and family history have contributed to their current dimensions of wellness. Based on this assessment, students will identify change and growth opportunities and strategies to promote a future wellness mindset.

title: Wellness: Career Design â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. Developing wellness through career design is important to one's overall well-being and success. The course centers around students coming to understand their personality, values, strengths, and interests and how they impact having a career of choice. It will ask them to look at appropriate sources of career information to get a sense of whether or not the requirements of the career (such as education level, major, and salary range) meet their values and allow them to live the life that they want to live. The course will additionally look at topics such as work/life balance, preparing job search materials and running an effective job search, offer evaluation, toxic work environments, and how to advance in one's career.

title: Wellness: The Power of Leading Yourself * â
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. A major part of wellness development and goal accomplishment in life is having the ability to lead oneself. A key aspect of this is becoming self-aware: knowing your values, your strengths, and weaknesses. This is also the first part of developing leadership for others. By guiding students through their self-reflection, this course helps them become better leaders.

title: Navigating Health and Wellness Scams â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This course will work to help students create a logical and scientific way to approach their health and evaluate health and wellness claims. A wide variety of examples of health and wellness products, influencers and advertising will be examined to help develop logic and critical thinking skills. Students will examine their beliefs and practices and use what they have learned in this course to help make educated wellness decisions moving forward.

title: Integrative Studies: Creativity â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors.  Students will focus on four aspects of creativity: the creative person, the creative process, the creative product, and the creative environment. Some degree of creativity is present in everyone, and according to many scholars, such creativity is teachable and learnable in a deliberate way. Thus, this course will explore multi-faceted nature of creativity and help students develop their own and others’ creativity and problem solving skills in areas of their expertise and interests .

title: Wellness: Thinking and Talking â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This Wellness course guides students in developing critical thinking and conversation skills. Curiosity, creativity, and courageous expression are the hallmarks of active minds, and healthy, cooperative thinking is fostered by affective engagement with others. Constructive conversation is a vital component of learning and wellness, and by facilitating meaningful dialogue, this course supports and promotes the continued wellbeing of students.

title: Integrative Studies: Crime and Justice â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. Students will examine the multi-faceted problems facing criminal justice in today's complex society through various theoretical lenses, with an eye toward problem solving and current policy issues. Among key topics of exploration are justice as a theoretical and practical concept, its application in laws and criminal code, and the role that legal and other actors from the criminal justice system play in democracy.

title: Integrative Studies: Values and Ethics â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. Speaking broadly, this course examines various historical concepts of wisdom, focusing on how best to integrate such concepts into daily and professional life. Students reflect back on their earlier learning,  and are asked to combine their personal experience with acquired knowledge, aimed at addressing real-life problems. More specifically, the course asks students to become familiar with important ethical  theories and concepts, and then apply such ideas to realistic case studies specifically designed and chosen for a college population.

title: Integrative Studies: Food, Science and Society â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. Students will apply interdisciplinary knowledge and experiential learning to the examination of the relationship between food and beverages, science, and society. Based on global foods past and present, we will discuss the implications for society and our understanding of science. We will investigate food preparation, cooking techniques, and/or fermented consumables, and the impact they have on culture, policies, and more. We will also examine the underlying biological, chemical, and physical concepts as we experiment with food and/or beverages.

title: Integrative Studies: Media Analysis â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. Organizations need to understand the media landscape in order to gain a thorough understanding of how to present their positions on critical issues. In this course, students will use media analyses to identify messages, examine how those messages are framed, and see how existing coverage of an issue could be improved.

title: Wellness: Occupations and Daily Activities â *
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This class will explore the intersection of wellness with occupations, both professional and recreational in nature. The course will provide students with an opportunity to explore their current occupations in relation to personal wellness and create a plan for future development that will influence their overall health and well being.

title: Integrative Studies: Leadership â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. This course will foster and integrate student understanding of leadership theory and concepts across an interdisciplinary and cross-cultural spectrum serving as a capstone course to the Keuka College general education Compass Curriculum requirement. Students will examine the elements of effective leadership in a variety of contexts through multiple lenses. Among key topics of exploration are definitions, roles and responsibilities, role models, skills and attitudes, leadership development, change making, ethical issues, and principle-based leadership. Students will develop an intellectual understanding of leadership and a working model for their own continued lifelong development.

title: Integrative Studies: Intercultural Studies â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. An interdisciplinary course designed to develop students' cultural self-awareness and intercultural competence through the application of concepts and perspectives drawn from a variety of academic fields (language and linguistics, communication, cultural studies, ethics, history, anthropology, sociology, psychology) to a range of problems and situations involving intercultural encounters. Students will consider their own culturally-based assumptions, biases and practices; articulate and analyze similarities and differences between cultures and subcultures; use both direct experience and technologies to enhance their knowledge of and experiences with intercultural encounters; consider ethical dimensions of cross-cultural encounters; and develop a problem-solving approach to improving communication and interactions with those from other cultural backgrounds. Through a deeper understanding of their own and other cultures, students will enhance their critical understanding of salient features of intercultural encounters.

title: Introductory Algebra
description: Required of all students achieving a score of 1 on the mathematics placement examination. An examination of the operations and properties of the real number system is followed by stress on algebraic expressions, simplification of expressions, solving equations, and verbal problems. This course is offered as pass/fail only with a grade of satisfactory (S) or unsatisfactory (U). A student placed in this course must achieve a grade of S prior to entry in any general education course in the area of mathematics. This course bears 3 billable credits to be applied to the student's semester course load. However, this is a developmental course and will not carry credit toward graduation or on student transcript.

title: Integrative Studies: Science in Popular Culture â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. For many people popular culture has a greater influence on how they view science than formal science education. This course will examine the relationship between science and popular culture. Using modern and historical examples from television, movies, books, games and other media, we will discuss the implications for society and our understanding of science.

title: Integrative Studies: Building A Culture of Health â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. This course undertakes in depth exploration of the internal and external factors that influence health in the United States. By taking a cross- disciplinary and health-focused approach, students are guided to critically examine social, genetic, technological, cultural, economic, and political factors that impact the health of a community. Employing concepts from the liberal arts, sciences, and technologies as well as evidence-based data, students will analyze cross-disciplinary factors relevant to health and community, and will develop proposals for effective community solutions and intervention strategies. Students will also be guided to apply course learning to individual health and reflect on ways to optimize their own personal culture of health.

title: Elementary Probability and Statistics â
description: Topics will include: display of data,  descriptive statistics, measures of central tendency, linear regression, counting, probability, random variables, expected value and variance of a random variable, discussion of gathering data, discrete probability distributions (Bernoulli, geometric, binomial), and hypothesis testing.

title: Pre-Law Field Period
description: Educational Field Period experience in a law office, judicial institution, court environment and/or related setting to gain experience in and learn more about the legal field. Involves final presentation and reflection.

title: College Algebra â *
description: Sets, real numbers, algebraic expressions, factoring, exponents, logarithms, solving algebraic equations, inequalities, exponential equations, radical equations, and applications.

title: Introductory Topics in Algebra â
description: This 1-credit course is designed to provide additional support to students who have gaps in their math backgrounds. Students who are given a math placement score of 1 and are registered for MAT 102 will be concurrently registered for this class. Key topics from the MAT-102 curriculum will be introduced pro-actively to build a foundation for the learning that will occur in MAT-102 . Topics will include, but are not limited to: exponents, factoring techniques, rational expressions, several varieties of equations, and systems of equations.

title: Wellness: Survive or Thrive Your College Experience * â
description: In this course, students explore the eight dimensions of wellness and develop a wellness mindset through the lens of the course topic. Through participation in experiential and problem-solving activities, students will acquire knowledge and skills that support the practice of wellness in daily life. Students will be guided, through self-assessment and reflection, to identify areas in their life where the practice of wellness will be most beneficial. Finally, students will develop an individualized wellness plan to positively impact self and others. Students may take up to three different Wellness topics for credit. This course serves as a bridge to college, to assist students in finding their strengths, resilience, and positivity, as well as explore how changes in their patterns of behavior contribute to their successes. Students begin and end this course with a simple choice, whether they want to survive college, or thrive in college.

title: Pre-Calculus â *
description: This course covers factoring techniques, operations with fractional expressions, and solving various types of higher level equations and inequalities; quadratic functions and circles; trigonometric functions, identities, equations, and applications; and logarithms.

title: Calculus III â
description: Parametric equations and polar coordinates, vectors and surfaces, vector-valued functions, differential calculus of functions of several variables, multiple integrals, vector calculus.

title: Calculus II â
description: Applications of integral calculus; techniques of integration including: (1) change of variables, (2) trigonometric substitution, (3) integration by parts, and (4) partial fractions, improper integrals, sequences, series, tests for convergence, Taylor's formula, Taylor series expansion of a function, and applications.

title: Integrative Studies: Culture, Gender, Community â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. This course will examine the cultural forces that shape our gender identity, to help them gain an understanding of important elements of the cultural contexts and historical development of gender in the United States and the importance of gender in communication problems we all experience in our personal relationships and careers. Students will identify communication problems they encounter, or expect to encounter in their personal and professional lives, analyze the role of gender in those problems and draw together insights from communication research that can help them envision solutions for those problems.

title: Calculus I â *
description: Limits and Continuity; Derivatives and their applications to Writing Equations of Tangent Lines, Graphing, Max/Min Problems, and Related Rates; Integrals and their applications to Finding Areas and Volumes.

title: Transition to Abstract Mathematics â
description: This course serves as a transition to abstract mathematics. Students will learn to read and write proofs. Topics may be selected from logic, sets, proof by induction, relations, functions, cardinality, combinatorics, number theory, graph theory.

title: Mathematical Concepts for Education II â
description: This course is the second in a sequence of two courses designed to prepare childhood, early childhood, and secondary special education majors to teach mathematics by developing a rigorous understanding of the mathematical content taught at those levels.  Topics will include: probability, Venn diagrams, logical reasoning, statistical measures, and various methods of statistical graphing, geometry, similarity, transformations, and constructions.  There will be emphasis on the vocabulary of mathematics and solving open ended problems using all of the skills covered in both MAT-221 and this course.

title: Linear Algebra â
description: Matrix algebra, vector spaces, subspaces, bases for vector spaces, determinants, solving linear systems, linear transformations, change of basis, eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and applications.

title: Integrative Studies: Social Justice in a Multicultural World â *
description: A writing-intensive capstone course for the Compass Curriculum that guides students in the application of interdisciplinary knowledge to complex problem solving and decision making to have a positive impact on a community. Students are challenged to practice critical and creative thinking skills, and they are expected to demonstrate competence in communication, collaboration, and engaged citizenship or leadership. This course is open to all majors. Students will be presented with diverse and evolving understandings of culture (as are represented in various disciplines/ways of knowing, ranging from those that are traditional and anthropologically sourced, to those that are emerging and based within post-modern theory) and, using these, will examine knowledge of themselves as individual culture beings. Students will explore how multiply - held societal and personal culture lenses influence the valuing and hierarchical determinations of culturally related elements. Students will be expected to demonstrate an understanding of ethical decision-making processes as they explore various modern social justice issues and the possible resulting effects of cultural determinations. Students will apply new understandings of culture and social justice to an exploration of present, ethical, social justice issues and determinations within their chosen disciplinary field.

title: Differential Equations â
description: This course examines aspects of the theory of ordinary differential equations and techniques of solving differential equations, including separable equations, exact equations, first order linear differential equations, and various second order linear differential equations. Linear second order differential equations with constant coefficients will be studied. Techniques for solving equations will include variation of parameters and the method of unknown coefficients. In addition, applications where differential equations model physical situations will be examined. Where appropriate, software will be used to help illustrate ideas and perform computations.

title: Foundations of Geometry â
description: Axiomatic Systems, finite geometries, advanced Euclidean concepts, plane hyperbolic geometry.

title: Probability â
description: Basic definitions of probability and rules of probability. Counting, discrete and continuous probability distributions. Discrete and continuous joint distributions of two random variables.

title: Abstract Algebra â
description: Algebraic structures, groups, rings, fields, and polynomial domains.

title: Senior Seminar â
description: Studies from selected areas of mathematics. Written reports and formal presentations will be required. This course is considered the capstone course for students completing the mathematics major. Students may repeat the course but not the topic.

title: Mathematics for Liberal Arts â
description: Specific topics will be selected, with a view toward answering the questions: What is mathematics? How have mathematical ideas developed through history? How might mathematics be relevant to everyday life? Topics will typically include items such as problem-solving, logic, numeration systems, number theory, mathematical finance, geometry, fractals, and probability.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Organizational Behavior â
description: This course covers the logical and rational design of organizations. There is also a thorough examination of behaviors within the individual and the group, and the effects of those interactions on the organization.

title: Introductory Topics in Management
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Management. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Mathematical Concepts for Education I â
description: This course is the first in a sequence of two courses designed to prepare childhood, early childhood, and secondary special education majors to teach mathematics by developing a rigorous understanding of the mathematical content taught at those levels. The course will cover the concepts of arithmetic, algebra, and number theory as they relate to the elementary and middle years mathematics curriculum. Topics will include: patterns, number sets, properties of real numbers, arithmetic algorithms for operations with all types of numbers, prime factorization and its applications, operations in binary systems, ratios, proportions, percents, measurements and conversion, linear equations, and using number theory to justify mathematical relationships.

title: Advanced Topics in Mathematics â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Mathematics. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Small Business Management
description: An analysis of the management concerns associated with small business. This course examines the means of ownership and characteristics of small business, as well as the benefits derived from and problems associated with managing small retail stores, service-oriented organizations, franchises, and manufacturing firms. Problems in marketing, finance, production, accounting, personnel, and insurance associated with the management of a small business are studied and analyzed. Development of a business plan is included.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Services Management and Marketing
description: Several businesses dominate the economics of most developed nations of the world. In the United States, the service sector employs over 75 percent of the workers and contributes over 70 percent of the GDP. Business courses have traditionally focused on manufacturing. This course introduces the student to the unique challenges faced by service organizations and by the growing number of manufacturing firms that use service as a competitive strategy.

title: Small Business Seminar
description: Analysis of issues related to small business, including accounting, data processing, marketing, management, legal matters, and funding.

title: Business and Government Relations
description: Analysis of governmental impact on business decisions; statutory requirements, regulation, and promotion of domestic and foreign business activities. Analysis of corporate political activity and current issues in business-government relations.

title: Management Internship
description: One-semester internship with a public or private organization provides opportunities for practical experience in management and observation of the concepts and the actual practice of managerial procedure.

title: Management Seminar
description: Analytical, ethical, and conceptual role of the manager in an organizational structure emphasizing the interrelationships of various managerial styles.

title: Real Analysis â
description: A rigorous treatment of the real number system and concepts from calculus. Specific topics include: the axioms of the real numbers, limits, sequences, series, convergence, continuity, differentiability, and the Riemann integral.

title: Advanced Topics in Management
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Management. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Leadership
description: This course will engage students in a critical examination of the changing nature of work and organizational conditions that create new challenges relating to the skills of business leaders. In addition to skills, development in the areas of goal setting, problem solving, and communications, this course will include assessment of personal leadership styles, decision-making and the capacity to function as an organizational change-agent. The ability to lead others through change will be the central theme of the course.

title: Managerial Accounting and Finance
description: This course is designed to provide applied competence in accounting and finance in the management of business organizations. The focus in on both how accounting measures can be used to promote efficient allocation of resources, and the ability to evaluate the business financing decisions in relation to their objectives. A case study approach will be taken.

title: Introductory Topics in Mathematics â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Mathematics. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Business Ethics
description: This course deals with morality in the business environment. It will deal with the centrality of ethical values to the individual and the organization, scope of the moral responsibilities of corporations, and ethical choices pervasive in management in the 21st century.

title: Managerial Economics
description: This course emphasizes the principles of economic theory which are essential to effective management decision-making. It reviews the fundamental principles of micro-economic and macro-economic theory. The focus is on: consumer demand; the nature and behavior of costs; the operation of markets; monetary theory and policy; financial institutions; employment and unemployment; capital formulation; and economic growth. A case study approach will be taken.

title: Strategic Thinking in Human Capital Management
description: This course introduces the student to the skills necessary to think strategically about business decisions regarding the ability to attract, employ, develop, motivate, engage, and retain strategically relevant talent.  This course presents the importance of aligning human resources with overall corporate strategy to establish a competitive advantage.  Understanding human capital needs is a primary drive for business success.  The course will focus on the current areas: understanding individual differences in the workplace; motivating and measuring performance; best practices in developing talent; how to engage the workforce and evaluate the culture; understanding the challenges in managing a global workforce.

title: Marketing for Managers
description: This course will focus on providing students with the wherewithal to conceptualize and develop an organization-wide marketing plan. The case-studies approach is designed to develop the necessary skills essential in making tactical and strategic marketing decisions.

title: Business Law
description: This course provides non-lawyer managers an overview of state and federal government regulatory agencies and regulations, intellectual property rights, contracts and tort laws, as it applies to businesses. Emphasis on liabilities issues.

title: Decision-Making for Managers
description: This course is designed to enable students to use various decision making models and techniques for more effective decision-making. Adult learners will be exposed to STAAR, statistical analysis, preferential analysis, decision trees, analytic hierarchy process, and conjoint analysis. Emphasis will be placed on group and team decision making. A case study approach will be taken.

title: Introduction to Research
description: This course will serve as an introduction to the rationale and procedures of research.  Topics include: nature and purposes of research, planning and conducting research, critique and evaluation of research, sampling, experimental and quasi-experimental design, and other research topics.  Students will develop critical skills needed as consumers and producers of research.  Content from this course will be integrated into all courses throughout the program.

title: Data Analysis for Research
description: A continuation of MGT 580. Students will become familiar with tools for statistical analysis. Students will design statistical measures that apply to their research project.

title: Healthcare Operations Management
description: This graduate-level course explores a system overview of healthcare services with a framework focused on operations management in healthcare. Special emphasis will be placed on performance improvement and quality management (Kaizen, lean hospitals, Six Sigma). Evidence-based medicine as a platform for healthcare delivery, use of technology (virtual care, medical home, home health), and balanced scorecard utilization will be covered in depth. Special project management, including a focus on telemedicine (tele-ophthalmology and tele-dermatology), will be covered in depth. Emphasis will be placed on effective services scheduling, capacity management, and supply chain issues (as seen during the COVID-19 pandemic). Emerging trends in healthcare delivery (e.g. tele-visits and reimbursement effects) will also be reviewed.

title: International Finance
description: A study of financial issues related to multinational business; the international financial environment; long term capital commitment to international ventures; and financial techniques for business operation.

title: Research Project
description: This is the culmination of a three-part comprehensive course running over the life of the program. This course provides instruction and support to students as they integrate the research information gained in previous course work and conduct and refine their projects. Students will share ideas, concerns and drafts of their final projects for peer review. Students will implement and complete their research and report their findings to a faculty review board.

title: Case Study in Financial Management
description: Using the case approach, this course examines financial issues including capital budgeting and long-term financing decisions. Topics include interaction of strategic investment (long-term assets) and financial decisions, projected cash flow, and risk analysis, required returns, alternative evaluation methods, capital structure consideration, and various financing methods.

title: International Marketing
description: A study of the opportunities, distinctive characteristics, and emerging trends in foreign markets. This course includes an exploration of alternative methods and control; impact of social, cultural, economic, and political strategies; organizational planning differences; and the challenges of adapting American marketing concepts and methods to foreign markets.

title: Enterprise Analytics and Information Management
description: This course introduces the student to the field of business analytics and an overview of data and information management. The topics covered in this course will include the following: the use of data warehouses, dashboards, key performance indicators, and data mining for making business decisions; an introduction to computer programming and computational thinking; an overview of information systems, their purpose, and their importance to international organizations. Computational thinking will introduce the student to the fundamental concepts of computer science used in problem solving, which include understanding human behavior, design, and algorithm development. Python, an object oriented programming language, will be used. Student learning will occur via individual and team projects.

title: Accounting Reporting and Analysis
description: A study of accounting measurements for general purpose financial reports. The course is to provide a working knowledge and a clear understanding of the contents of published financial statements.

title: International Accounting
description: A study of accounting concepts, standards, and procedures for the international business environment. International and national accounting pronouncements affecting international business with be emphasized.

title: Business Strategy and Industry Analysis
description: As the capstone course within the Masters of Science in Leadership program, this course focuses on understanding what elements go into building a successful enterprise and creating a winning business strategy. Conversely, industrial analysis answers the question: what makes one industry more attractive than another for investment and employment? The focus is on developing the ability to formulate innovative strategies to create and sustain competitive advantages in a dynamic business environment. Tools for industry analysis will be introduced such as benchmarking. A business simulation and the creation of a business plan will be incorporated into this course.

title: Federal Taxes
description: This course is an introduction to federal taxation in general, and income tax in particular. It will concentrate on the provisions that apply to all taxpayers, with particular concern for the taxation of individuals. The course is intended to provide grounding in such fundamental areas as the concept of income, income exclusions and exemptions, basic tax accounting, assignment of income and capital gains and losses.

title: Auditing
description: An overview of the public accounting profession and its socioeconomic role; auditing techniques with an emphasis on EDP environment; audit procedures, practice and programs; working paper preparation; unqualified and qualified report writing.

title: Human Resource Management for International Business
description: Consideration of the nature and significance of human relations as they affect the performance of the international business organization. Topics include group and inter-group relations, leadership, motivation, and communication systems. Relevant readings from psychology and social sciences are incorporated.

title: Employee Training and Development
description: Increasing employees' capability to perform more efficiently, effectively and with greater productivity can often differentiate one company from another. The employee training and development course teaches students about the process of improving individual performance in organizations. Students will adopt the perspective of a training and development department that is responsible for the optimum contribution of all employees. Students will be challenged to apply their understanding of competency-based development strategies. Students can expect to learn the fundamentals of how to: conduct job analysis; assess trainees; develop competency-based training and evaluate training effectiveness.

title: International Management
description: The study of the legal impact of the various social institutions on international managers. The study of the role of culture, education, and politics in shaping attitudes toward work and leisure. The study of the role of discipline and motivation in different cultures.

title: Introductory Topics in Marketing
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Marketing. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Advanced Topics in Management Information Systems
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Management Information Systems. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Enterprise Information Systems and Technology
description: This course introduces the student to the field of information systems and technology.  The topics covered in this course will include the following:  information systems, hardware and software, social media, data management, data analytics, information security, and the strategic use of information systems.  A particular focus of the course will be how technology is applied for competitive advantage, and used to enhance other disciplines such as marketing, accounting, finance, and operations.

title: Principles of Marketing
description: An introduction to marketing. Topics covered include marketing's pervasive growth, marketing management, planning, implementation and control. The student is introduced to many aspects of marketing, more specifically consumer behavior, retailing, public relations, Internet marketing, and Social media marketing. Attention is focused on marketing's ongoing evolution amid emerging technologies in a global context.

title: Public Relations
description: An introduction to Public Relations (PR) and its growing importance in the public, private, and non-profit sectors of the economy. We explore how PR is planned and implemented for targeted results over a wide range of media - focusing especially on the growing use of social and mobile media. Students will produce their own PR campaign including PR messages over diverse media, ranging from news releases to short videos for the Internet.

title: Social Media Marketing
description: Social media technologies are continuously transforming the way in which consumers interact with each other and firms. These transformations constitute a fundamental shift in the marketplace - consumers have greater opportunities to voice their opinions and connect with other consumers and have an increased influence over marketers and brands. As a result, the conventional approaches to marketing communications have become more and more challenged. This puts an added emphasis on leveraging social media to engage consumers and propagate ideas, messages, products, and behaviors. This course takes an in-depth look at the relationship between social media and human behavior, and examines how organizations capitalize on social media, and these consumer-to-consumer interactions, to support their marketing efforts. Students will get hands-on experience creating comprehensive social media strategies for active brands and designing ideas that spread.

title: Marketing Research and Data Analytics
description: Linking Data to Business Decisions: This course focuses on managing the marketing research process, which provides information as an input to marketing decision-making. This requires an understanding of the components of the marketing research process, how to utilize it effectively to obtain relevant information, and how to integrate such information into the marketing decision-making process. We will also discuss leveraging data and analytics to drive managerial decisions and marketing strategy.

title: Content Marketing
description: In this course, you will learn the core strategies content marketers use to acquire and retain customers profitably. Specifically, you will learn how to develop, organize and implement a content marketing strategy, analyze and measure the effectiveness of content marketing, write compelling copy, use a strategic framework when writing, and build your professional brand and authority through content marketing. You will also learn how to put the ideas presented to you into action and build your own personal brand through content marketing.

title: Principles of Advertising
description: An analytical view of the role of advertising in society and business. Emphasis is placed on the creation, planning, and budgeting of promotional campaigns, methods of how advertising informs buyers, promotes sales, and persuades individuals to become consumers.

title: Principles of Digital Marketing
description: Explore the digital marketing environment from both a consumer and business perspective. The course provides an overview of various online business models and delves into digital advertising and social media marketing techniques and technologies. Course will include digital marketing strategy, inbound marketing, social media, mobile, web-based, and SEO/SEM.

title: International Marketing
description: This course introduces the student to the field of multinational marketing, with emphasis placed on international competition; distribution systems; pricing and credit policies; promotional methods; trade barriers; trade agreements; and the areas of the political, legal, economic, cultural, and ethical problems faced in attempting to market in foreign nations.

title: Career Management and Development
description: This is a comprehensive course, where students first learn about themselves and how that knowledge contributes to career success. They use various career resources to investigate careers and discover how goal setting and decision marketing are integral to career planning. They will learn how to use this knowledge in a managerial context to assist organizations in managing their workforce for maximum individual and organizational success.

title: Consumer Behavior
description: Consumer decision- making in the marketplace; research in psychology, sociology, and other behavioral fields; present and potential applications for marketing will be examined.

title: Introductory Topics in Management Information Systems
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Management Information Systems. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Graduate Project
description: Introduction to the sequence of steps followed during the identification and delineation of a research project that leads to the successful development of a study of international business. The complete research project includes introduction, review of literature, research methodology, analysis and findings, and conclusion and recommendations.

title: Strategic Marketing Capstone
description: The Capstone is the culminating experiential project in both the BS Marketing & BS Digital Marketing degree programs.  It consists of a live case study project in which students will actively engage in a real-world marketing consultancy with a client.

title: Advanced Topics in Marketing
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Marketing. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Music History â *
description: An overview of the history and cultural significance of music through the ages. A survey of classical and popular music with an emphasis on the European classical tradition and American Jazz and popular style periods within the context of world music. This course looks at the techniques, trends, people and places that have contributed to the evolution of music.

title: Chorale â
description: A traditional choral ensemble that rehearses and performs a wide-range of music styles.  Open to all students regardless of ability and experience.  Chorale performs one or two concerts per semester.  May be repeated for credit.  A maximum of six semester credit hours may be applied toward graduation.

title: American Musical Traditions â
description: This course is intended to help students think critically and creatively about music, with a focus on a range of American musical traditions-- popular as well as classical. Attention will be paid to the global roots (most notably European, African, and Latin American musical traditions) and variety of styles and forms of American music (minstrelsy, tin pan alley, musical theater, jazz, blues, folk and bluegrass, rock, rap, hip-hop), and to the influence of technology and mass media on the development of music within the United States from Stephen Foster to Gershwin to Ellington, from Elvis to a remarkably diverse contemporary musical landscape.

title: Introductory Topics in Music â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Music. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Band â
description: A creative instrumental ensemble open to all student musicians on any instrument.  The band performs one or two concerts per semester in mostly jazz and popular styles. May be repeated for credit. A maximum of six semester credit hours may be applied toward graduation.  The group meets for three hours each week.

title: Advanced Topics in Music â
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Music.  Intended for juniors and seniors or for students with an appropriate background in the subject.  The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Orientation to Professional Practice II
description: This interdisciplinary seminar course provides an overview of the foundational concepts and principles of health and human services. It aims to introduce students to the fundamental knowledge, skills, and practice benefits of an interdisciplinary approach to care. By integrating various perspectives, students will gain an introductory understanding of the roles, responsibilities, and collaborative nature of the professions in promoting health, well-being, and functional independence.

title: Orientation to Nursing Professional Practice I
description: This seminar course introduces nursing students to the major. Students will engage in learning activities to promote success in the program by familiarizing themselves with career options related to the major. The course will include a broad overview of current issues related to healthcare in the United States as well as provide a survey of concepts and practice areas related to nursing.

title: Health Assessment
description: This course provides theory and laboratory practice of basic health assessment skills for individuals across the life span. The student uses the nursing process as a framework to develop interviewing, health history assessment, and documentation skills to conduct a systematic, comprehensive health assessment.

title: Introductory Topics in Nursing
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Nursing. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Lab: Nursing Fundamentals
description: Course Laboratory Component

title: Marketing Principles in Global Business
description: Marketing is an important business function and a key to the success of every company.  This course explores the emergent application of technology that will allow students to market in more efficient ways, while maintaining that the classic elements of product, price, placement and promotion still hold true. In this course we explore the maxim that when carefully applied, technology will improve the efficiency of, but not the basic nature of, marketing.  By the end of this course, students will have the following knowledge and skills: 1. Understanding marketing concepts and strategies. 2.	Knowing the importance of customer needs and wants as the foundation for marketing decision-making. 3.	Knowing the processes and techniques used in marketing decision-making. 4.	Understanding the complexity of marketing decision-making in light of economic, societal and ethical demands.

title: Epidemiology
description: This course presents basic epidemiological concepts and methods with emphasis on the health status and health needs of a population, on the levels of prevention, and on the promotion of health strategies. General methods introduced include epidemiological measures, measures of effect, sources of data, descriptive components, study designs and strategies for screening, and surveillance in the community. Practical applications for health care providers are emphasized.

title: Clinical: Nursing Fundamentals
description: Course Clinical Component

title: Nursing Research
description: This course is an introduction to the principles of scientific inquiry in nursing. Students are guided in the development of critical appraisal skills in the evaluation of both quantitative and qualitative research methodologies and in the application of research findings to nursing practice. Resources appropriate for nurse researchers are explored with the opportunity to define a research problem and to initiate a literature search.

title: Genetics in Nursing
description: This course provides the nurse of the future with a basic understanding of the principles of human genetics. Major content areas include the human genome project, cross-cultural issues in human genetics, and genetic conditions across the life span. Students begin to evaluate the inherent ethical dilemmas that this expanding knowledge poses for modern health care providers.

title: Family Health Nursing
description: This initial nursing course introduces Keuka's community-based curriculum with a client family as the primary unit of investigation. Using a family developmental life-cycle approach and a family systems framework, the evolution and growth of many types of families is traced over time. Through a systematic approach to family assessment, students analyze the cultural, ethnic, racial, religious, and socio-economic variables that influence family life in this rural community. Critical thinking is encouraged as nursing interventions are developed and implemented with consideration given to the utilization of community resources to enhance family functioning.

title: Acute Care of the Adult I
description: This is a theory and practicum course which explores concepts of acute and chronic nursing care including but not limited to disorders of the cellular, cardiovascular, respiratory, immune, gastrointestinal, renal, hormonal regulation, metabolism, and reproductive systems. Professional role development will also be addressed to ensure a solid educational foundation. Basic concepts of medical-surgical nursing will be applied to adult clients across the healthcare continuum.

title: Pathophysiology and Pharmacology
description: This course explores the pathophysiology of select acute and chronic disease processes across the lifespan using a systems approach.  With a focus on the major body systems, students will analyze clinical manifestations of disease and their relationship to pathophysiological processes.  Concepts of pharmacotherapy, including precision medicine are discussed.  Use of technological tools to maximize patient safety and identify potential quality issues related to pharmacotherapy is introduced.  Non-pharmacological, complementary, and/or alternative therapies are explored as both treatment modalities and enhancers of wellness.  Holistic patient education is emphasized.

title: Holistic Health Assessment Across the Lifespan
description: This is a theory and practicum nursing course which explores holistic health assessment and professional role development to ensure a solid educational foundation. Using the nursing process as a framework, the student learns and practices holistic interview, physical assessment, and documentation skills. Special emphasis is placed on reflective practice and a collaborative team approach to provide holistic care across the lifespan.

title: Clinical: Acute Care of the Adult I
description: Course Clinical Component

title: Historical and Theoretical Foundations
description: This course explores the history of nursing, nursing theories, and the current practice of professional nursing worldwide.  Leaders and innovators in the field of nursing are studied within their historical context, and emphasis is placed on their impact on current nursing practice.  Selected nursing theories are examined and appraised.  Current trends and issues in the profession are identified and analyzed.  Professional ethics and responsibilities are examined, and means to attain lifelong professional nursing citizenship are formulated.  The meaning of nursing as a caring profession is examined, and a personal mission, vision and philosophy of nursing is developed.

title: Nutrition and Metabolism
description: This theory course builds upon knowledge gained from previous science courses to deepen the student's understanding of energy metabolism and physiologic and pathophysiologic processes that interfere with these metabolic processes. Dietary needs for persons experiencing various physiologic and pathophysiologic stressors are explored. Nutrigenomics and nutrigenetics are discussed and reflect diversity of susceptibility to disease and the role of nutrition in the prevention and management of disease.

title: Nursing History and Theory
description: This is a theory based nursing course which explores the history of nursing, nursing and related theories, and professional role development. Leaders and innovators in the field of nursing are studied within their historical context. Current trends and issues in the profession are identified and analyzed.

title: Bioenergetics and Metabolism
description: This course provides an in-depth study of the chemical structure and function of biomolecules (nucleic acids, proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) as well as vitamins/cofactors, enzymes, hormones, and their roles in bioenergetic processes and the regulation of metabolic pathways. Epidemiological principles and ecological factors that guide appropriate screening and education, as well as evidence-based approaches to the care of persons experiencing disorders of metabolism will be explored.

title: Nursing Fundamentals
description: This is a theory and practicum nursing course which explores fundamental nursing concepts and professional role development to ensure a solid educational foundation. Through the use of the nursing process, the student will interact and perform basic nursing and communication skills with diverse human families in a variety of laboratory and health care settings.

title: Mental Health Nursing
description: This is a theory and practicum nursing course which explores mental health concepts and professional role development to ensure a solid educational foundation. Nursing’s unique body of knowledge is used to apply and implement evidence-based care for persons (individuals, families and communities) with mental health issues, including psychosocial concepts; cultural, ethical, spiritual and legal influences.

title: Maternal Child Health
description: This is a theory and practicum course which explores maternal-child nursing concepts and professional role development to ensure a solid educational foundation. Nursing’s unique body of knowledge is used to implement evidence-based care for persons (individuals, families and communities) during antepartum, intrapartum and postpartum periods.

title: Clinical: Pediatric Nursing
description: Course Clinical Component

title: Pathophysiology and Pharmacology
description: This is a theory based nursing course which explores the pathophysiology of select disease processes across the lifespan. Using a systems approach, the student will be immersed in the concepts of pharmacotherapeutics and its safe and effective use in the treatment of common acute and chronic diseases. Non-pharmacologic integrative therapies are also explored. Special emphasis is placed on reflective practice and a collaborative team approach to provide holistic care across the lifespan.

title: Community Health Nursing
description: This course focuses on the theory and practice of community health nursing. Community health nursing is a blend of two components: public health science, with its roots in epidemiology, and the art and science of nursing. While students work with individuals and families, emphasis is placed on the community-as-client, critically examining the population-focused practice of nursing in a rural community. In partnership with the community, students analyze current assessment data to develop a proposal to meet an identified community need. Clinical learning experiences occur in a variety of community-based settings.

title: Nursing Research and Evidence-Based Practice
description: This course is an introduction to the principles of nursing research and evidence-based practice.  Building on a liberal arts foundation, students learn principles of scientific inquiry and subsequent application to clinical practice.  All phases of research methodology are explained and interpreted, and scholarly research as a foundation for safe and effective clinical practice is examined.  Current models of evidence-based practice are compared, and use in the clinical setting assessed.  Critical appraisal skills are developed as students critique quantitative and qualitative nursing research.  Use of digital technology as a research tool is explored.  Implications of the research process and principles of evidence-based practice for professional nursing practice are explored.

title: Holistic Health Assessment
description: This course provides experience in the theory and practice of holistic health assessment of patients across the lifespan.  With an emphasis on normal findings, the student practices skills of patient interview, physical assessment and documentation.  The goal of completing a systematic health assessment is accomplished through both didactic course content and hands-on experiential learning.  Students conduct a comprehensive health history and perform a head to toe physical assessment.  Supervised practice sessions allow the student to further develop assessment skills.  Technological advances in patient assessment are explored.  Patient safety strategies, inter-professional collaboration and effective communication skills are explored and applied, and holistic assessment of the patient’s social and environmental milieu is emphasized.

title: Pediatric Nursing
description: This is a theory and practicum nursing course which explores the care of children and professional role development to ensure a solid educational foundation. Nursing’s unique body of knowledge is used to apply evidence-based care to the pediatric population. Partnering with caregivers, students integrate concepts of wellness and primary prevention, as well as, the care of acute and chronic conditions.

title: Nursing Leadership
description: This course focuses on the nurse's leadership roles in the rapidly changing global health care environment. Course content includes group theory and development, models of leadership, organizational theory and development, change theory, and conflict management. Managed care concepts, quality improvement, and the evaluation of patient care outcomes are also highlighted. Seminar discussions enhance understanding of business processes and of the political, legal, and ethical ramifications of core topics.

title: Professional Practice Issues
description: In this capstone senior seminar, students are encouraged to critically analyze societal trends and health issues that influence professional nursing practice. Students provide leadership for selecting, planning, implementing, and evaluating seminars. Knowledge and skills from other nursing courses are integrated as the student is challenged to explore current employment opportunities and to make projections for the changing roles of the nurse in the 21st century. Individual student portfolios are completed during this course.

title: Population Health
description: This course investigates the theory and practice of population health.  Students identify the myriad of health beliefs, values, and practices of families and communities, and recognize the importance of culturally specific care.  Basic epidemiological concepts and methods are examined, with emphasis on the health status and health needs of a population, levels of prevention, and health promotion strategies.  General methods introduced include epidemiological measures, sources of data, descriptive components, strategies for screening, and surveillance in the community.  The science of public health nursing and its contributions to health at the local, state, national, and global levels are analyzed.  Current global health initiatives are examined.  Emphasis is placed on the community-as-client to critically evaluate a population-within a geographic community, along with application to nursing practice.

title: End of Life
description: This is a theory based course which explores end-of-life issues with a cultural perspective. These quality of life issues include physical, psychological, and spiritual aspects of dying. Ethical and legal issues, public policy and individual considerations at the end of life are examined. Special emphasis is placed on reflective practice and a collaborative team approach to provide holistic care across the lifespan.

title: Basic Organizational Systems Leadership
description: Building on a liberal arts foundation, this course focuses on the role of the nurse as both leader and manager in a rapidly changing global health care environment.  Relevant theories, scholarly evidence, industry best practice standards, and practice exemplars that support the creation of a positive work environment for safe and high-quality patient care are explored and applied, and basic business and entrepreneurial skills are integrated.  Using relevant data, analysis of the health care landscape within a global context is emphasized.  Topics include: theories and models of leadership, industry business trends and skills, organizational and change theories, political, legal and ethical ramifications of leading patient care, budgeting, marketing, planning, problem solving and decision making.  Strategies to maximize effective group work from a leadership perspective are identified.

title: Population Health Nursing
description: This is a theory and practicum nursing course which explores the care of populations and professional role development to ensure a solid educational foundation.
