The focus is on population health with specific emphasis on two components: population health science, with its roots in epidemiology and the art and science of nursing within the community setting. Special emphasis is placed on reflective practice in a collaborative team approach to provide holistic care across the lifespan.

title: Clinical: Acute Care of the Adult II
description: Course Clinical Component

title: Clinical: Maternal Child Health
description: Course Clinical Component

title: Evidenced-Based Practice and Nursing Research
description: This is a theory based nursing course which explores principles of nursing research, evidence based practice, and professional role development to ensure a solid educational foundation. The student learns principles of scientific inquiry and its application to nursing practice.

title: Clinical: Mental Health Nursing
description: Course Clinical Component

title: Acute Care of the Adult II
description: This is a theory and practicum course where students examine increasingly complex concepts of acute and chronic nursing care. Professional role development is explored to ensure a solid educational experience. Advanced approaches to medical-surgical nursing in critical care areas will be applied to adult clients across the healthcare continuum.

title: Foundational Concepts of Nursing Leadership and Healthcare Management
description: This is a theory based nursing course which focuses on concepts related to the role of the nurse as a leader and manager of care in a rapidly changing environment. This course focuses on evidence based strategies that foster quality patient care across the care continuum. Analysis of the healthcare landscape within a global context is explored. Special emphasis is placed on reflective practice using a collaborative interdisciplinary team approach to provide holistic care across the lifespan. Topics include: models of leadership, industry business trends and skills, organizational and change theories, political legal and ethical ramifications of leading patient care, budgeting, marketing, planning, problem solving and decision making.

title: Clinical: Population Health Nursing
description: Course Clinical Component

title: Nursing Field Period and Senior Seminar I
description: This course centers around experiential learning, and enables students to build on generalist education as they integrate new knowledge and skills.  Throughout the course, students will build on growth in nursing program outcomes, while also attaining professional nursing proficiencies.  Real world application of principles of leadership, inter-professional collaboration to enhance quality of patient care and safety (QSEN), and a systems approach are emphasized.  Students begin this course at the onset of the nursing program through development of their nursing Field Period experience, and spend 140 hours fulfilling the objectives of their Field Period contract. Through subsequent participation in a senior level seminar, students provide evidence of attainment of baccalaureate level nursing competencies through application their Field Period learning.  Incorporation of evidence based practice, relevant health care policy, and information technology is expected as the student presents documentation of growth in the nursing graduate outcomes, and through creation of a professional mission, vision, values and goal statement along with a personal plan for lifelong learning and professional citizenship.

title: Nursing Field Period
description: This course is based in experiential learning and enables students to build on generalist education as they integrate new knowledge and skills while meeting program outcomes and attaining professional nursing competencies. Under the direction of nursing faculty, students design and implement a college and community health fair. Nursing students work with faculty and community agencies to schedule, advertise, design and implement health screening and health education interventions for the college and local communities. There is opportunity to work with faculty and students in other disciplines. Health screening examples include: blood pressure screening, blood glucose screening and lipid profiling. Health education examples include nutrition and stress management techniques. Application of principles of leadership, inter-professional collaboration to enhance the health of college and community residents, and a systems approach are emphasized as students develop, plan, and implement interventions. Students begin this course with development of a field period contract, spend 150 hours fulfilling the objectives of their contract, and ultimately share their field period experience in seminar format in the classroom setting. Incorporation of evidence-based practice, relevant health care policy, and information technology is expected.

title: Health Care Policy: Advanced Practice Nurse
description: This course provides the student with a greater understanding of the health of the world population. The course begins with an overview of the political aspects of the healthcare delivery system, including the relevance of the political process in nursing, and information describing how one can become informed. Social, political, and economic considerations are discussed within the delivery of global healthcare. Health policy is studied in relation to relevant theories and principals of patient centered care. Research and theory are integrated as students consider potential strategies for change as leaders within the profession of nursing. Issues discussed include the controversy over the appropriate educational preparation for entry into practice. Information about the role of law and policy are explained in relation to legal issues in nursing practice. Leadership and management principles are further developed to give the masters prepared nurse the tools needed to perform in an international leadership role.

title: Nursing Informatics
description: This course extends and integrates the nurse's knowledge of nursing science, nursing interventions and evidenced based practice with computer science, informatics science and technology. Students examine and critique a variety of healthcare information sources and technologies focused on improving patient care outcomes by navigating and evaluating their reliability and validity. Patient care is considered relative to technology teaching tools, particularly as it aims to improve standards of care. The utilization and management of various information and communication technologies will be evaluated with attention to the ethical and legal ramifications. Those technologies that are most appropriate to enhance the delivery of patient care and to improve patient outcomes will be identified.

title: Evaluation of Adult/Gerontology Health for the Advanced Practice Nurse
description: This course focuses on the assessment of adult health status and application of clinical reasoning and evidence-based practice in the management of health status deviations.  Clinical reasoning using advanced health assessment skills and knowledge of advanced path-physiology and pathopharmacology leads to the formulation of comprehensive differential diagnosis. Use of appropriate diagnostic tools and timely referral is explored.  Formulation of treatment plan using best evidence and system resources is introduced.  Emphasis on professional role socialization and functioning within the interdisciplinary health care team prepares the practitioner for the advanced practice role within the current health care system.  Scope of practice and legal aspects  of the advanced practice role are examined including New York State and federal laws related to prescriptions and record-keeping.

title: Advanced Pharmacology
description: Using a systems approach, this course provides the adult learner with the opportunity to develop critical thinking skills and share knowledge related to the management of common pathophysiologic processes occurring within diverse populations across the lifespan. Systems to be explored include: renal, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, endocrine, neurological, musculoskeletal and integumentary. Concepts of pharmacotherapy are presented including pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, and pharmacogenomics. In addition, non-pharmacologic, complementary, and/or alternative therapies are explored. Economic implications and their effects on the ethics of healthcare decision making are discussed and explored. Identification and management of co-morbidities will be addressed, with a strong emphasis on the importance of support from caregivers and / or family. Availability of community resources is explored. The adult learner is expected to engage in experiential hands-on learning. The adult learners have an opportunity to apply theoretical knowledge and evidence based knowledge while practicing health promotion strategies in selected community settings.

title: Advanced Pathophysiology
description: Using a systems approach, this course provides the adult learner with the opportunity to develop critical thinking skills and share knowledge related to the management of common pathophysiologic processes occurring within diverse populations across the lifespan. Systems to be explored include: cardiac, vascular, respiratory, and immune. Concepts of pharmacotherapy are presented including pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, and pharmacogenomics. In addition, non-pharmacologic, complementary, and / or alternative therapies are explored. Economic implications and their effects on the ethics of healthcare decision making are discussed and explored. Identification and management of co-morbidities will be addressed, with a strong emphasis on the importance of support from caregivers and / or family. Availability of community resources is explored. The adult learner is expected to engage in experiential hands-on learning. The adult learners have an opportunity to apply theoretical knowledge and evidence based knowledge while practicing health promotion strategies in selected community settings.

title: Professional Seminar/Capstone
description: This course integrates and applies knowledge gained through the core and specialty courses as well as internship/clinical experiences. Current issues and concepts affecting the role of the masters prepared nurse are explored relative to their ability to affect change. Research and theory are integrated as students propose potential strategies for change as leaders within the profession of nursing. The comprehensive exam will be an indicator of having met program outcomes.

title: Education Internship
description: This course provides the opportunity for the direct application of teaching / learning theory, classroom and clinical instruction as well as outcome measurement in a self-selected student teaching experience. Adult learners are encouraged to select a setting that is consistent with their future professional goals as an educator. The internship experience must include a minimum of 80 hours of combined instructional and evaluation preparation. The opportunity for experiential and service learning is provided as students accept accountability and apply concepts learned. Professional and personal development is encouraged throughout the experience as continuous self-evaluation occurs.

title: Advanced Health Assessment
description: This course provides the adult learner with the opportunity to develop advanced health assessment skills. Using the nursing process, the adult learner will obtain and interpret health histories and conduct thorough physical examinations with diverse populations across the lifespan. Normal aging is compared to and contrasted with pathological history and examination findings. An introduction to the process of diagnostic reasoning is provided. Health promotion and risk reduction concepts are stressed throughout the course. Primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention strategies are explored in light of the various predictors of health (i.e. genomics, socioeconomic situation, culture, and spiritual beliefs). The adult learner is expected to engage in experiential hands-on learning. The students have an opportunity to apply theoretical knowledge and evidence-based knowledge while practicing health assessment skills in assigned teams.

title: Orientation to Professional Practice I
description: This seminar course introduces occupational therapy (OT) students to the major.  Students will engage in introductory learning activities to promote success in the program by familiarizing themselves with career options related to the major.  The course will include a broad overview of current issues related to healthcare in the United States as well as provides a survey of concepts and practice areas related to occupational therapy.

title: Occupational Science â
description: This course will examine the principles of occupational science and the use of occupation to establish meaning and competence. Students will understand how people establish identity through the development of the occupational self. In addition, they will apply the strategies of foundational task and activity analysis to occupations in context.

title: Orientation to Professional Practice II
description: This interdisciplinary seminar course provides an overview of the foundational concepts and principles of health and human services. It aims to introduce students to the fundamental knowledge, skills, and practice benefits of an interdisciplinary approach to care. By integrating various perspectives, students will gain an introductory understanding of the roles, responsibilities, and collaborative nature of the professions in promoting health, well-being, and functional independence.

title: Occupation and Theories of Practice
description: This course provides a unique exploration into the foundational principles of occupational science, introduction to the occupational therapy practice framework, and theories of practice in occupational therapy. Students will explore the use of occupation to establish meaning and competence and understand how people establish identity through the development of the occupational self. Students will recognize the influence of occupation on well-being and quality of life through understanding occupation-based practice while analyzing current theories, models and frames of reference which shape practice.

title: Introductory Topics in Occupational Science
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Occupational Science. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Clinical Kinesiology
description: The focus of this course is on musculoskeletal anatomy and biomechanics of human motion. Primary emphasis is placed on functional anatomy of the extremities, head, neck, and trunk. Laboratory focus is on palpation, manual muscle testing and goniometry

title: Nursing Field Period and Senior Seminar II
description: This course centers around experiential learning, and enables students to build on generalist education as they integrate new knowledge and skills.  Throughout the course, students will build on growth in nursing program outcomes, while also attaining professional nursing proficiencies.  Real world application of principles of leadership, inter-professional collaboration to enhance quality of patient care and safety (QSEN), and a systems approach are emphasized.  Students begin this course at the onset of the nursing program through development of their nursing Field Period experience, and spend 140 hours fulfilling the objectives of their Field Period contract. Through subsequent participation in a senior level seminar, students provide evidence of attainment of baccalaureate level nursing competencies through application their Field Period learning.  Incorporation of evidence based practice, relevant health care policy, and information technology is expected as the student presents documentation of growth in the nursing graduate outcomes, and through creation of a professional mission, vision, values and goal statement along with a personal plan for lifelong learning and professional citizenship.

title: Organizational and Systems Leadership
description: This course provides an overview of the advanced nursing roles and responsibilities in the healthcare system. The differentiated needs of the client being served and the range of specialized services offered will be explored. The course begins with an overview of the economic and financial aspects of the healthcare delivery system, including specific issues across the system and their relevance to nursing, healthcare finance and control, and the evolving role of the masters prepared nurse. Utilizing research and technology, the masters prepared nurse assesses changes in healthcare delivery informed by the principles of related theories. Integration of such advanced understanding is analyzed related to the ability to deliver evidence-based and value- based nursing care. Ethical considerations and legal professional responsibilities surrounding the delivery of healthcare are explored in an ever-changing environment.

title: Nursing Theory and Research
description: This course presents a foundation for understanding the discipline and development of nursing knowledge as an essential component of scholarly practice for the Masters Prepared Nurse. This course provides the student with a better understanding of how evidence-based practice is translated into the healthcare environment. The research process is presented as students develop an appreciation for the thought processes used to formulate a research problem and purpose. Quantitative, qualitative, and outcomes research are examined in an effort to investigate a variety of research problems and purposes. Published studies are separated into their component parts to evaluate the logical consistency of the theoretical system and its interaction with the study methodology. Quantitative, qualitative, and mixed methods of research are reviewed through performance of critiquing skills. Methodology, implementation, and data analysis/evaluation are stressed as the processes of statistical analyses are presented. Relevant theories highlight the application of nursing research aimed at providing evidence-based nursing care across a variety of patient care settings. The interrelationship of nursing theory, research, and practice is explored using best practice models. This course includes the theoretical basis of outcomes research, a brief history of emerging attempts to examine outcomes, the importance of outcomes research designed to examine nursing practice, and methodologies used in outcomes research.

title: Occupational Engagement Across the Lifespan
description: This course explores the intricate relationship between human development and occupational engagement from infancy through end of life. Drawing on theories and research from occupational therapy, psychology, sociology, and other relevant disciplines, students will gain a comprehensive understanding of how occupational engagement evolves and impacts individuals' overall well-being. By exploring the interplay between biological, psychological, social, and environmental factors, students will develop a nuanced perspective on the complex dynamics of occupational development. Students will understand factors that influence individuals achieving meaningful and fulfilling lives through engagement in occupations that are congruent with their developmental needs and goals.

title: Health Promotion and Occupational Participation
description: Students will explore behavioral interventions for lifestyle choices related to occupational participation. This course will define and cover areas such as interventions focusing on evidence-based approaches, medical management, engagement strategies, planning health care for individuals, groups and populations with or at risk for chronic health conditions.

title: Lab: Clinical Kinesiology
description: Lab for OCC-333 .

title: Lab: Psychopathology and Occupation
description: Lab for OCC-343 .

title: Pediatric Medical Application
description: The Pediatric Medical Application course will explore pediatric assessment in the medical and rehabilitation setting. Analysis or appropriate use and interpretation of assessments along with evidence-based treatment techniques from a range of theoretical frames of reference is used. Experiences through laboratory assignments along with clinical reasoning and use of case studies are included; lecture and laboratory.

title: Psychopathology and Occupation
description: An integrated theory and evidence based practice course examining occupational therapy psychosocial treatment approaches in a variety of settings. Areas explored will include clinical skills, social determinants, behavioral health systems. This course will also introduce techniques for prevention, remediation of occupation dysfunction, maintenance of competence, group processes, clinical program design and adaptations related to psychosocial dysfunction.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Level II Fieldwork I
description: The student will complete the first of two Fieldwork experiences totaling 24 weeks of full-time employment/clinical experience under the direct supervision of a registered occupational therapist serving a variety of ages and diagnoses. The type of clinical setting or environment is a collaborative effort between the fieldwork coordinator, the student, and the clinical site supervisor. The student learning objectives are developed by the fieldwork sites with review by Academic Fieldwork Coordinator. The primary emphasis for this fieldwork is the student's transition from academic learner to entry-level practitioner. The student must translate the academic knowledge base to actual clinical practice in a professional manner. Registration in this course constitutes full-time attendance. A student may begin Level II Fieldwork only after completing all the specified academic requirements. Keuka College requires a minimum of 24 weeks Level II Fieldwork.

title: Determinants of Health Across the Lifespan
description: This course introduces students to the underlying causes of health, wellness, and illness. Theories and evidence supporting multiple underlying determinants of health are presented through the lens of common conditions that are seen in occupational therapy practice across the lifespan. Through these perspectives, students will increase their understanding of the interplay of biology, genetics, individual behavior, health services, social factors, and policymaking that influence the health of individuals and populations.

title: Theory Development and Application
description: Development of philosophy and theory in occupational therapy; examination of the conceptual models which have shaped occupational therapy since its inception, analysis of current theories, models  and frames of reference which shape practice and scholarship, in-depth analysis of the concepts underlying occupational behavior, occupational science and clinical reasoning.

title: Occupations Across the Lifespan
description: This course will develop the student's skill in understanding and applying the principles of occupation as they related to the health and wellness of individuals. Through lecture, discussion, application activities, and experiential learning, students will explore the occupations engaged in by individuals across the lifespan, with a focus on occupational balance and adaptation in response to life changes. This course will integrate analysis and application of literature from the discipline of occupational science.

title: Therapeutic Documentation
description: This course provides occupational therapy students with essential skills and knowledge to effectively document client care and meet professional standards. This includes an overview for future occupational therapists on medical record documentation requirements and professional, responsible documentation strategies. The course will cover therapeutic requirements in various practice settings, reimbursement systems, funding and appeals mechanisms, and treatment/diagnosis codes that affect occupational therapy.

title: Level II Fieldwork II
description: The student will complete the second of two Fieldwork experiences totaling 24 weeks of full-time employment/clinical experience under the direct supervision of a registered occupational therapist serving a variety of ages and diagnoses. The type of clinical setting or environment is a collaborative effort between the fieldwork coordinator, the student, and the clinical site supervisor. The student learning objectives are developed by the fieldwork sites with review by Academic Fieldwork Coordinator. The primary emphasis for this fieldwork is the student's transition from academic learner to entry-level practitioner. The student must translate the academic knowledge base to actual clinical practice in a professional manner. Registration in this course constitutes full-time attendance. A student may begin Level II Fieldwork only after completing all the specified academic requirements. Keuka College requires a minimum of 24 weeks Level II Fieldwork.

title: Therapeutic Approaches to Psychosocial Function
description: This course focuses on the development of skills and the occupational therapy process that support clientsâ engagement in the occupations of daily life across the lifespan. This integrated theory and practice course examines occupational therapy treatment approaches for psychosocial dysfunction. Students will develop a comprehensive understanding of evaluation and intervention approaches related to a variety of occupational performance needs. These approaches may include: create, promote (health promotion), establish, restore (remediation, restoration), maintain, modify (compensation, adaptation), and prevent (disability prevention) on the basis of clientsâ desired outcomes.

title: Lab: Therapeutic Approaches to Psychosocial Function
description: Lab for OCC-430

title: Pediatric Community Application
description: The Pediatric Community Application course will explore pediatric assessment in the community including the educational setting and early intervention. Analysis of appropriate use and interpretation of assessments along with Evidence-Based techniques from a range of theoretical frames of reference is used. Experience through lab assignments along with clinical reasoning and use of case studies are included; lecture and laboratory.

title: Practice Skills I
description: This laboratory course focuses on the development of skills and therapeutic techniques that support clients' engagement in the occupations of daily life.  Students will develop assessment and intervention skills and related competencies for: wheelchair seating and positioning of adults and pediatric populations, mobility (both community and other functional areas), eating and feeding for adults and pediatric populations (including dysphagia management), home evaluation and safety, work and productivity, and ergonomics.  Students will integrate occupational therapy theories and frames of reference as these apply to an understanding of the clinical skills taught in this course.

title: Lab: Pediatric Community Application
description: Lab for OCC-441

title: Lab: Pediatric Medical App
description: Lab for OCC-341

title: Adult Medical Application
description: The Adult Medical Application course will explore adult assessment in the medical and rehabilitation setting. Analysis of appropriate use and interpretation of assessments along with evidence-based treatment techniques from a range of theoretical frames of reference is used. Experiences through laboratory assignments along with clinical reasoning and use of case studies are included; lecture and laboratory.

title: Therapeutic Approaches to Physical Function
description: This course focuses on the development of skills and the occupational therapy process that support clientsâ engagement in the occupations of daily life across the lifespan. This integrated theory and practice course examines occupational therapy treatment approaches for physical dysfunction. Students will develop a comprehensive understanding of evaluation and intervention approaches related to a variety of occupational performance needs. These approaches may include: create, promote (health promotion), establish, restore (remediation, restoration), maintain, modify (compensation, adaptation), and prevent (disability prevention) on the basis of clientsâ desired outcomes.

title: Adult Community Application
description: The Adult Community Application course will explore adult assessment in the community including topics outpatient care and home health. Analysis of appropriate use and interpretation of assessments along with Evidence-Based techniques from a range of theoretical frames of reference is used. Experience through lab assignments along with clinical reasoning and use of case studies are included; lecture and laboratory.

title: Therapeutic Approaches to Neurocognitive Function
description: This course focuses on the development of skills and the occupational therapy process that support clientsâ engagement in the occupations of daily life across the lifespan. This integrated theory and practice course examines occupational therapy treatment approaches for neurocognitive dysfunction. Students will develop a comprehensive understanding of evaluation and intervention approaches related to a variety of occupational performance needs. These approaches may include: create, promote (health promotion), establish, restore (remediation, restoration), maintain, modify (compensation, adaptation), and prevent (disability prevention) on the basis of clientsâ desired outcomes.

title: Enhancing Occupational Engagement
description: This laboratory course focuses on the development of skills and therapeutic techniques that support clientsâ engagement in the occupations of daily life across the lifespan. Students will develop evaluation and intervention skills related to functional mobility, the use of assistive technology and physical agent modalities to enhance treatment and promote client outcomes. Students will be able to design, fabricate, apply, fit and train in orthotics and prosthetic devices. Utilizing these approaches, students will enhance the clientsâ desired occupational outcomes.

title: Managerial Roles
description: The focus of this course is on the application of current literature in management and leadership to the roles of a manager, consultant, and leader within traditional and non-traditional practice settings. Students will actively participate in developing skills relating to human resources, budgeting, and management.

title: Occupational Science Field Period I Traditional
description: This is an experiential Field Period designed for the senior occupational therapy students to practice evaluation, treatment planning, and intervention skills learned the previous semester in the occupational therapy courses.  This traditional site experience is designed to meet 1.5 credits which is 1/2 of the credit requirements or 70 hours of an experiential learning opportunity per academic year that a student attends Keuka College.

title: Principles of Practice I
description: This course promotes studentsâ ability to demonstrate the ethical integration of clinical concepts learned throughout their didactic coursework to increase independent and critical thinking skills concurrently while on Level II Fieldwork experiences. Students will engage in opportunities to enhance understanding of evidence-based, inter/intradisciplinary, theory-driven and occupation-centered evaluation and intervention.

title: Advanced Topics in Occupational Science
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Occupational Science. Intended for juniors and seniors or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Level II Fieldwork
description: The student will complete 12 weeks of full-time employment/clinical experience under the direct supervision of a registered occupational therapist. The type of clinical setting or environment is a collaborative effort between the fieldwork coordinator, the student and the clinical site supervisor. The student learning objectives are developed by the fieldwork sites with review by the Division of Occupational Therapy. The primary emphasis for this fieldwork is the student's transition from academic learner to entry-level practitioner. The student must translate the academic knowledge base to actual clinical practice in a professional manner. Registration in this course constitutes full-time attendance. A student may begin Level II Fieldwork only after completing all the specified academic requirements. It consists of working full-time for 24 weeks in occupational therapy clinics, serving a variety of ages and diagnosis. Keuka College requires that three months be completed in a setting serving those with physical dysfunction. A student may choose to complete a third Level II placement in an area of interest, which may be two or three months long.

title: Scholarship in Occupational Therapy II
description: This course is the follow-up to OCC 521, and will further facilitate the application of knowledge and skills related to scholarship, research, and the use of evidence in OT practice. Class meetings will include lecture, discussion groups, peer presentations, and learning activities integrating scholarship and the use of evidence with students’ clinical experiences and future scholarly interests. Students will continue to work with an assigned faculty mentor on small-group, faculty directed scholarly activities. Students will compile, integrate, and share the results of this collaboration with peers, faculty, and other professionals.

title: Practice Skills II
description: This course focuses on the development of advanced clinical skills and clinical reasoning by focusing on some of the most challenging areas of occupational therapy practice: treatment of clients with spinal cord injuries and complex medical diagnoses such as burns, cancer and kidney disease. Through this treatment lens, students will explore such topics as client sexuality, end of life issues, and the use of assistive technology to enhance treatment and promote client outcomes. Students will integrate occupational therapy theories and frames of reference such as the Model of Human Occupation as they apply to an understanding of the clinical skills taught in this course. This course will be presented in a seminar format.

title: Level II Fieldwork
description: The student will complete 12 weeks of full-time employment/clinical experience under the direct supervision of a registered occupational therapist. The type of clinical setting or environment is a collaborative effort between the fieldwork coordinator, the student and the clinical site supervisor. The student learning objectives are developed by the fieldwork sites with review by the Division of Occupational Therapy. The primary emphasis for this fieldwork is the student's transition from academic learner to entry-level practitioner. The student must translate the academic knowledge base to actual clinical practice in a professional manner. Registration in this course constitutes full-time attendance. A student may begin Level II Fieldwork only after completing all the specified academic requirements. It consists of working full-time for 24 weeks in occupational therapy clinics, serving a variety of ages and diagnosis. Keuka College requires that three months be completed in a setting serving those with physical dysfunction. A student may choose to complete a third Level II placement in an area of interest, which may be two or three months long.

title: Contemporary Issues
description: This course is designed to provide students with an in-depth exploration of contemporary issues in practice. It aims to stimulate contemplation regarding the standards of practice and ethical challenges with an emphasis on the most recent available evidence and clinical reasoning for addressing professional issues. Students will engage in cutting-edge topics, trends, and challenges that shape the professional practice in occupational therapy.

title: Interdisciplinary Practice
description: This course offers a dynamic and intellectually stimulating journey through the interconnected world of various health care disciplines. Students will learn to perceive their profession as an integral part of an inter-professional team, and to assimilate the responsibilities and approaches for continuous professional advancement. This course equips students with the essential knowledge, skills, and attitudes required to function effectively as members of inter-professional teams. It encompasses research and theories that provide guidance for successful team formation and resolution of problems .

title: Community Outreach and Advocacy
description: This course will provide occupational therapy students with the knowledge and skills necessary to actively engage in community-based initiatives, influence policy development, and advocate for the profession. These activities will provide opportunities for linking evidence-based knowledge within advanced practice situations and demonstrate collaboration with communities/organizations and other professionals.

title: Integration Lab Across the Lifespan
description: This course is designed to integrate the concepts and skills gained throughout the professional education program by having the student complete formal presentations and expanded written assignments of patient case studies. Emphasis will be placed on evaluating, the OT assessment and treatment plan development process in a holistic manner, which includes the patients' physical, psychological, and social situation. Treatment alternatives selected will be validated through the use of professional literature.

title: Lab: Therapeutic Approaches to Neurocognitive Function
description: Lab for OCC-450

title: Clinical Capstone
description: This course will focus on development of critical reasoning skills through a variety of assessments.  These exercises will provide opportunities for linking evidence based knowledge within advanced practice situations. The course will also address education, preparation for professional certification and licensure.

title: Advanced Clinical Application
description: This course aims to enhance students' existing knowledge by exploring specialized aspects of occupational therapy practice. It will equip them with the essential skills and comprehension needed to tackle intricate challenges in the field. Students will actively participate in collaborative discussions, case-based problem-solving, and hands-on exercises to apply the acquired concepts. By the course's conclusion, students will have acquired advanced knowledge, honed critical thinking abilities, and developed professional competencies that will empower them to excel in occupational therapy and make contributions to the profession.

title: Practice Excellence
description: This seminar course is designed to integrate the concepts and skills gained throughout the duration of the professional education program and experiential learning to enhance clinical reasoning skills through patient case studies and clinical simulations across the lifespan and practice settings. The focus will be on conducting a comprehensive, evidence-based OT assessment and intervention process that takes into account the patient's overall well-being, including their physical, psychological, and social circumstances.

title: Principles of Practices II
description: This course promotes studentsâ ability to demonstrate the ethical integration of clinical concepts learned throughout their didactic coursework to increase independent and critical thinking skills concurrently while on Level II Fieldwork experiences. Students will engage in opportunities to enhance understanding of evidence-based, inter/intradisciplinary, theory-driven and occupation-centered evaluation and intervention.

title: Scholarship in Occupational Therapy I
description: This course is designed to facilitate the application of knowledge and skills related to scholarship, research, and the use of evidence in OT practice. Class meetings will include lecture, discussion groups, peer presentations, and learning activities built around the generation and application of scholarly knowledge. Students will engage in the development of a research proposal on a self-selected topic, and will identify areas for future scholarship. In addition, students will participate in small-group, faculty-directed scholarly activities, designed to build knowledge and skills while engaging students in a range of scholarly activities relevant to OT practice.

title: Group and Organizational Dynamics â
description: This course is a study of group behavior and how group functioning affects organizational effectiveness with emphasis on decision-making and conflict resolution. Adult learners develop strategies for efficient and productive group management and determine which tasks are handled by groups or individuals.

title: Advanced Topics in Organizational Management
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Organizational Management. Intended for juniors and seniors or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Leadership & Management of Self and Others
description: This course has a goal of fostering a deeper comprehension of healthcare management, leadership, entrepreneurial principles, and ethical challenges encountered in the occupational therapy (OT) field. Emphasis will be placed on evidence-based practice and clinical reasoning skills in addressing professional matters, thereby enhancing students' understanding of their own roles as leaders and managers, both personally and in relation to others. As part of managing self, the course aims to cover various aspects such as education surrounding professional certification, and licensure preparation.

title: Personal Values and Organizational Ethics â
description: In this module, adult learners discover and define their own beliefs, values, purpose, moral philosophy, and ethical principles. No particular view or perspective will be prescribed. Because expressing and hearing disagreement are essential team skills, the course also provides adult learners with opportunities to practice skills that will transfer to the workplace.

title: Introduction to Philosophy â *
description: The historical examination of philosophical thinking and philosophers, emphasizing value judgments, the meaning of life, and the quest for personal identity and wisdom.

title: Action Research Project
description: The Action Research Project is a major research effort designed to apply the action research model to adult learners' work locations. This module serves as the capstone to the Organizational Management Program by providing a platform for application of knowledge and skills acquired within an organization's changing environment. This course enhances knowledge in an area related to one's work or community, improves writing and presentation skills, and develops, research skills to assist in decision making.

title: Independent Study
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

title: Advanced Topics in Physical Education
description: An advanced study of a specific topic within Physical Education. Intended for juniors and seniors, or for students with an appropriate background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Ethics â *
description: Foundations, presuppositions, and meaning of morality; major ethical theories and standards of conduct from Greek origins to the present; applications of ethical theories and principles to selected areas of human concern and action.

title: Introductory Physics â *
description: A survey of the major topics and themes in physics. Topics will include forces, motion, electricity and magnetism, waves, optics, and sound. The lab will reinforce these concepts and show their applications.

title: Introductory Topics in Philosophy â
description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Philosophy. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Environmental Ethics â
description: This course examines the numerous moral issues that arise from studying the environment, integrating both classical and modern ethical theory. The course also examines the critical roles played by diverse communities in addressing these critical environmental issues.

title: Independent Study â
description: An in-depth, self-directed study for Junior- or Senior- level students under the guidance of a sponsoring faculty member on a planned topic that is not covered by an existing Keuka College course.

description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Organizational Management. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.

title: Organizational Communication â
description: This module investigates the role of communication in creating a productive organizational environment. It aids adult learners in developing and strengthening their communication skills by focusing on interpersonal, group and presentation skills.

title: General Physics II â
description: Principles of oscillatory motion, waves, sound and fluids. Coverage of topics from: the principles of electricity and magnetism, elementary DC and AC circuits, electromagnetic waves, geometric and wave optics, and modern physics; lecture and laboratory.

title: Lab: Introductory Physics
description: Lab for PHY-110

title: Lab: General Physics II
description: Lab for PHY 202.

title: General Physics I â
description: Principles of vectors, mechanics, statics, dynamics, work, energy and momentum, rotational kinematics and dynamics; lecture and laboratory.

description: An introductory study of a specific topic within Physical Education. Intended for freshmen and sophomores, or for students with little or no background in the subject. The student may repeat the course, but not the topic.
